title.zh: 卷六十二 荀韓鍾陳列傳
title.en: Volume 62: Biographies of Xun, Han, Zhong, Chen
title.raw: 卷六十二 荀韓鍾陳列傳 Volume 62: Biographies of Xun, Han, Zhong, Chen

sentences.zh: 荀淑字季和，穎川穎陰人

translations.literal: Xun Shu, courtesy name Jihe, was a man of Yingyin in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Shu, styled Jihe, came from Yingyin in Yingchuan Commandery.

translations.literal: (The final particle ye 也, marking assertion.)
translations.idiomatic: (This particle closes the clause identifying him; English absorbs it into the preceding sentence.)

sentences.zh: ，荀卿十一世孫也。

translations.literal: , he was the eleventh-generation descendant of Xun Qing.
translations.idiomatic: He was the eleventh-generation descendant of Xun Qing.

sentences.zh: 〈卿名況，趙人也。

translations.literal: 〈Xun Qing's given name was Kuang; he was a man of Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Xun Qing's personal name was Kuang; he was a native of Zhao.

sentences.zh: 為楚蘭陵令。

translations.literal: He served as magistrate of Lanling in Chu.
translations.idiomatic: He held office as magistrate of Lanling in the state of Chu.

sentences.zh: 著書二十二篇，號荀卿子。

translations.literal: He wrote twenty-two treatises, and was styled Master Xun Qing.
translations.idiomatic: He authored twenty-two works and came to be known as Master Xun.

sentences.zh: 避宣帝諱，故改曰「孫」也。〉

translations.literal: To avoid the taboo on Emperor Xuan's name, [the clan name] was therefore altered to say 'Sun.'〉
translations.idiomatic: To honor the taboo on Emperor Xuan's name, the character used for the clan was therefore written as 'Sun.'〉

sentences.zh: 少有高行，博學而不好章句，多為俗儒所非，而州里稱其知人。

translations.literal: In youth he had lofty conduct; he was broadly learned but did not favor textual glossing, and was often criticized by vulgar scholars, yet his locality praised his ability to know men.
translations.idiomatic: From an early age he showed exemplary conduct. Though erudite, he disdained fussy textual philology; commonplace scholars often faulted him for it, while people in his district praised his judgment of character.

sentences.zh: 安帝時，徵拜郎中，後再遷當塗長。

translations.literal: Under Emperor An he was summoned and appointed Gentleman of the Palace, and later was twice promoted to magistrate of Dangtu.
translations.idiomatic: During Emperor An's reign he was called to court as a Gentleman of the Palace and later rose twice in rank to become magistrate of Dangtu.

sentences.zh: 〈當塗，縣名，故城在今宣州。〉

translations.literal: 〈Dangtu is the name of a county; its old walled town is in present-day Xuanzhou.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Dangtu was a county; its former seat lies in what is now Xuanzhou.〉

sentences.zh: 去職還鄉里。

translations.literal: He left office and returned to his home village.
translations.idiomatic: He resigned his post and returned home.

sentences.zh: 當世名賢李固、李膺等皆師宗之。

translations.literal: Leading worthies of the age such as Li Gu and Li Ying all took him as their teacher and honored him.
translations.idiomatic: Contemporary luminaries—Li Gu, Li Ying, and others—all revered him as their master.

sentences.zh: 及梁太后臨朝，有日食地震之變，詔公卿舉賢良方正，光祿勳杜喬、少府房植舉淑對策，譏刺貴倖，為大將軍梁冀所忌，出補朗陵侯相。

translations.literal: When the Dowager Empress Liang held court, there were portents of solar eclipse and earthquake; an edict ordered the nobles to recommend men of integrity and moral excellence. The Superintendent of the Imperial Household Du Qiao and the Privy Treasurer Fang Zhi recommended Shu for the examination; he satirically attacked the powerful favorites and was resented by the Grand General Liang Ji, and was therefore posted out as chancellor to the Marquis of Langling.
translations.idiomatic: When Empress Dowager Liang held the reins of government, eclipses and earthquakes prompted an edict calling on high officials to recommend worthy and upright candidates. Du Qiao, Superintendent of the Imperial Household, and Fang Zhi, Privy Treasurer, nominated Shu for the policy examination. His answers skewered powerful favorites; the Grand General Liang Ji took offense and had him sent out as chancellor to the Marquis of Langling.

sentences.zh: 〈《續漢書》曰：淑對策譏刺梁氏，故出也。

translations.literal: 〈The Xu Hanshu says: In his examination answers Shu satirized the Liang clan, hence he was sent out.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈According to the Xu Hanshu, Shu's policy critique targeted the Liang family, which explains his relegation to a provincial post.〉

sentences.zh: 馬事明理，稱為神君。〉

translations.literal: In handling affairs he was clear and principled, and was called the 'divine magistrate.'〉
translations.idiomatic: As magistrate he governed with clarity and integrity and was hailed as the 'divine lord.'〉

sentences.zh: 頃之，弃官歸，閒居養志。

translations.literal: Before long he abandoned office and returned, living in retirement to cultivate his aims.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward he quit office and retired home to pursue his studies in seclusion.

sentences.zh: 產業每增，輒以贍宗族知友。

translations.literal: Whenever his estates increased, he would use [the surplus] to provide for his clan and friends.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever his income grew, he shared it with kinsmen and friends.

sentences.zh: 年六十七，建和三年卒。

translations.literal: At age sixty-seven he died in the third year of the Jianhe era.
translations.idiomatic: He died at sixty-seven in Jianhe 3.

sentences.zh: 李膺時為尚書，自表師喪。

translations.literal: Li Ying was then Minister of the Imperial Secretariat and personally petitioned [the throne] regarding mourning for his teacher.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ying, then a Secretary in the Imperial Secretariat, memorialized to observe mourning for his teacher.

sentences.zh: 〈《禮記》曰「事師無犯無隱，左右就養無方，服勤至心，心喪三年」也。〉

translations.literal: 〈The Book of Rites says: 'In serving a teacher, neither offend nor conceal; attend him on either side without fixed rule; labor with utmost devotion; observe heart-mourning for three years.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Liji states that one serves a teacher without giving offense or holding back, cares for him without rigid formulas, and labors with full devotion—mourning him in heart for three full years.〉

sentences.zh: 二縣皆為立祠。

translations.literal: Both counties established shrines for him.
translations.idiomatic: Both counties erected shrines in his honor.

sentences.zh: 有子八人：儉，緄，靖，燾，汪，爽，肅，專，並有名稱，時人謂[之]「八龍。」

translations.literal: He had eight sons: Jian, Kun, Jing, Tao, Wang, Shuang, Su, Zhuan, all of whom had reputations; people of the time called them the 'Eight Dragons.'
translations.idiomatic: He fathered eight sons—Jian, Kun, Jing, Tao, Wang, Shuang, Su, and Zhuan—each widely known; contemporaries dubbed them the "Eight Dragons."

sentences.zh: 〈緄音昆。

translations.literal: 〈Kun is read like the character kun 昆.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The name Kun is pronounced like the character 昆.〉

sentences.zh: 燾音道。

translations.literal: Tao is read like dao.
translations.idiomatic: The character 燾 is pronounced dao.

sentences.zh: 汪音烏光反。

translations.literal: Wang is read wu-guang fan (fanqie spelling).
translations.idiomatic: Wang is spelled with the fanqie initial wu and final guang.

sentences.zh: 《說文》云：「汪，深廣也。

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: 'Wang means deep and vast.'
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen jiezi glosses the graph wang as meaning deep and vast.

sentences.zh: 」俗本改作「注」，非。

translations.literal: ' In vulgar editions it is wrongly altered to zhu.'
translations.idiomatic: Vulgar editions wrongly substitute the character zhu; that is incorrect.

sentences.zh: 「專」本或作「敷」。〉

translations.literal: 'Zhuan' in some manuscripts is written 'fu.'〉
translations.idiomatic: Some manuscripts write his name with fu instead of zhuan.〉

sentences.zh: 初，荀氏舊里名西豪，

translations.literal: Formerly the old village of the Xun clan was called West Hao,
translations.idiomatic: Long ago the Xun family's old neighborhood was known as West Hao,

sentences.zh: 〈今許州城內西南有荀淑故宅，相傳云即舊西豪里也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Inside the southwest part of Xuzhou city today is Xun Shu's former residence; tradition says it is the old West Hao village.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈In the southwest of modern Xuchang city stands Xun Shu's former home, traditionally identified with old West Hao.〉

sentences.zh: 穎陰令勃海苑康以為昔高陽氏有才子八人，

translations.literal: the magistrate of Yingyin, Yuan Kang of Bohai, thought that in antiquity the Gaoyang clan had eight talented men,
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Kang of Bohai, magistrate of Yingyin, recalled that the Gaoyang lineage once boasted eight gifted men,

sentences.zh: 〈《左傳》曰：「昔高陽氏有才子八人：蒼舒，隤敳，檮戭，大臨，尨降，庭堅，仲容，叔達。

translations.literal: 〈The Zuozhuan says: 'The Gaoyang clan had eight talented men: Cang Shu, Tui Ai, Tao Yin, Da Lin, Pang Jiang, Ting Jian, Zhong Rong, Shu Da.'
translations.idiomatic: 〈As the Zuozhuan records, the Gaoyang line produced eight paragons: Cang Shu, Tui Ai, Tao Yin, Da Lin, Pang Jiang, Ting Jian, Zhong Rong, and Shu Da.

sentences.zh: 」今荀氏亦有八子，故改其里曰高陽里。〉

translations.literal: ' Now the Xun clan also has eight sons, so [Yuan Kang] changed the village name to Gaoyang Ward.'〉
translations.idiomatic: ' Since the Xuns likewise had eight distinguished sons, he renamed the district Gaoyang Ward.'〉

sentences.zh: 靖有至行，不仕，年五十而終，號曰玄行先生。

translations.literal: Jing had the utmost conduct; he did not take office, died at fifty, and was titled Master Profound Conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Jing lived an exemplary life without entering office; he died at fifty and was remembered as Master Profound Conduct.

sentences.zh: 〈皇甫謐《高士傳》曰「靖字叔慈，少有俊才，動止以禮。

translations.literal: 〈Huangfu Mi's Gaoshi zhuan says: 'Jing, courtesy name Shuci, had outstanding talent in youth and regulated movement and rest by ritual.'
translations.idiomatic: 〈Huangfu Mi's Biographies of High-Minded Men records that Jing, styled Shuci, showed precocious talent and kept every gesture within the bounds of ritual.

sentences.zh: 靖弟爽亦以才顯於當時。

translations.literal: Jing's younger brother Shuang also displayed talent in that age.
translations.idiomatic: His younger brother Shuang likewise won renown in their day.

sentences.zh: 或問汝南許章曰：『爽與靖孰賢？

translations.literal: Someone asked Xu Zhang of Runan: 'Which is worthier, Shuang or Jing?'
translations.idiomatic: When someone asked Xu Zhang of Runan whether Shuang or Jing was the better man,

sentences.zh: 』章曰：『皆玉也。

translations.literal: ' Zhang said: 'Both are jade.'
translations.idiomatic: Xu answered, 'Both are flawless jade.'

sentences.zh: 慈明外朗，叔慈內潤。

translations.literal: 'Ciming is bright without; Shuci is lustrous within.'
translations.idiomatic: 'Ciming shines outwardly; Shuci glows from within.'

sentences.zh: 』及卒，學士惜之，誄靖者二十六人。

translations.literal: ' When [Jing] died, scholars lamented him; twenty-six men composed dirges for Jing.'〉
translations.idiomatic: When Jing died, scholars mourned him deeply—twenty-six men contributed elegies for him.'〉

sentences.zh: 穎陰令丘禎追號靖曰玄行先生」也。〉

translations.literal: The magistrate of Yingyin, Qiu Zhen, posthumously styled Jing Master Profound Conduct.'〉
translations.idiomatic: Qiu Zhen, magistrate of Yingyin, posthumously honored him as Master of Profound Conduct.'〉

sentences.zh: 淑兄子昱字伯條，曇字元智。

translations.literal: Shu's nephew Yu, courtesy name Botiao, and Tan, courtesy name Yuanzhi.
translations.idiomatic: Shu's nephews—Yu, styled Botiao, and Tan, styled Yuanzhi—

sentences.zh: 昱為沛相，曇為廣陵太守。

translations.literal: Yu served as chancellor of Pei, Tan as governor of Guangling.
translations.idiomatic: Yu became chancellor of the Pei kingdom and Tan governor of Guangling.

sentences.zh: 兄弟皆正身疾惡，志除閹宦。

translations.literal: The brothers both corrected themselves and hated evil, intending to eliminate the eunuchs.
translations.idiomatic: Both brothers kept their conduct upright, loathed corruption, and resolved to purge the eunuch faction.

sentences.zh: 其支黨賓客有在二郡者，纖罪必誅。

translations.literal: Their faction's retainers and guests who were in the two commanderies—any petty crime was surely punished.
translations.idiomatic: Any associate or client of their faction found in either jurisdiction was executed for the slightest offense.

sentences.zh: 昱後共大將軍竇武謀誅中官，與李膺俱死。

translations.literal: Yu later joined Grand General Dou Wu in plotting to execute the palace officials, and died together with Li Ying.
translations.idiomatic: Yu later conspired with Grand General Dou Wu to destroy the inner court eunuchs and perished alongside Li Ying.

sentences.zh: 曇亦禁錮終身。

translations.literal: Tan was also barred from office for life.
translations.idiomatic: Tan was permanently blacklisted from holding office.

sentences.zh: 爽字慈明，一名諝。

translations.literal: Shuang, courtesy name Ciming, also called Xu.
translations.idiomatic: Shuang, styled Ciming, also bore the name Xu.

sentences.zh: 〈音息汝反。〉

translations.literal: 〈It is read xi-ru fan.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Pronounced with the fanqie spelling xi–ru.〉

sentences.zh: 幼而好學，年十二，能通《春秋》、《論語》。

translations.literal: In youth he loved learning; at twelve he could master the Chunqiu and the Lunyu.
translations.idiomatic: He loved books from childhood; by twelve he had mastered the Spring and Autumn Annals and the Analects.

sentences.zh: 太尉杜喬見而稱之，曰：「可為人師。

translations.literal: The Grand Commandant Du Qiao saw him and praised him, saying: 'He can be a teacher of men.'
translations.idiomatic: Grand Commandant Du Qiao, impressed, declared that he was fit to instruct others.

sentences.zh: 」爽遂耽思經書，慶吊不行，征命不應。

translations.literal: ' Shuang thereupon immersed himself in the classics; he did not attend congratulations or condolences, and did not respond to summonses.
translations.idiomatic: Shuang thereupon buried himself in the classics, avoided social calls of congratulation and condolence, and ignored every imperial summons.

sentences.zh: 穎川為之語曰：「荀氏八龍，慈明無雙。」

translations.literal: Yingchuan had a saying about him: 'Among the eight dragons of the Xun clan, Ciming has no peer.'
translations.idiomatic: A Yingchuan proverb ran: "Of the Xun clan's Eight Dragons, none rivals Ciming."

sentences.zh: 延熹九年，太常趙典舉爽至孝，拜郎中。

translations.literal: In the ninth year of Yanxi, the Minister of Ceremonies Zhao Dian recommended Shuang for supreme filial piety, and he was appointed Gentleman of the Palace.
translations.idiomatic: In Yanxi 9, Minister of Ceremonies Zhao Dian nominated Shuang for outstanding filial devotion, and he received appointment as a Gentleman of the Palace.

sentences.zh: 對策陳便宜曰：

translations.literal: In his examination answers he set forth what was advantageous, saying:
translations.idiomatic: In his policy response he offered practical recommendations:

sentences.zh: 臣聞之於師曰：「漢為火德，火生於木，木盛於火，故其德為孝，

translations.literal: I have heard from my teacher: 'Han possesses the virtue of Fire; fire is born from wood, and wood flourishes in fire; therefore its virtue is filial piety,'
translations.idiomatic: I learned from my teachers that the Han claims the Fire phase: fire is born of wood, yet wood in turn thrives amid fire—hence its ruling virtue is filial devotion,

sentences.zh: 〈火，木之子；

translations.literal: 〈Fire is the child of wood;〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Fire is the child of the Wood phase;〉

sentences.zh: 夏，火之位。

translations.literal: Summer is the position of fire.
translations.idiomatic: Summer corresponds to the Fire position.

sentences.zh: 木至夏而盛，故為孝。

translations.literal: Wood reaches its fullness in summer, hence it is filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: Wood reaches its peak in summer; that abundance embodies filial piety.

sentences.zh: 其像在周易之離。〉

translations.literal: Its image is Li in the Zhouyi.〉
translations.idiomatic: The Zhouyi images this relationship in the trigram Li (Fire).〉

sentences.zh: 」夫在地為火，在天為日。

translations.literal: ' Among earthly forms it is fire; among heavenly bodies it is the sun.'
translations.idiomatic: On earth it appears as fire; in the sky it is the sun.'

sentences.zh: 〈易說卦曰「離為火，為日」也。〉

translations.literal: 〈The Yi's Shuogua says: 'Li is fire; it is the sun.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The "Explanation of the Trigrams" states that Li stands for fire and for the sun.〉

sentences.zh: 在天者用其精，在地者用其形。

translations.literal: What is in heaven employs its subtle essence; what is on earth employs its forms.
translations.idiomatic: The celestial aspect manifests as refined qi; the terrestrial aspect takes visible form.

sentences.zh: 夏則火王，其精在天，溫暖之氣，養生百木，是其孝也。

translations.literal: In summer fire is king; its essence is in heaven; the warm qi nurtures the hundred trees—this is its filiality.
translations.idiomatic: In summer Fire holds sway: its subtle power fills heaven, and the warm air quickens every growing thing—that is its "filial" nurturing.

sentences.zh: 冬時則廢，其形在地，酷烈之氣，焚燒山林，是其不孝也。

translations.literal: In winter it is debilitated; its form is on earth; fierce qi burns mountains and forests—this is its unfiliality.
translations.idiomatic: In winter that virtue fails: its harsh form scorches hill and forest—an "unfilial" destructiveness.

sentences.zh: 故漢制使天下誦孝經，選吏舉孝廉。

translations.literal: Therefore Han institutions made the realm recite the Xiaojing, and in selecting officials recommended filial and incorrupt men.
translations.idiomatic: Thus Han law required the realm to study the Classic of Filial Piety and promoted officials through the "filial and incorrupt" recommendation.

sentences.zh: 〈平帝時，王莽作書八篇戒子孫，令學官以教授，吏能誦者比《孝經》。

translations.literal: 〈In Emperor Ping's time Wang Mang composed eight admonitory chapters for his descendants and ordered the academicians to teach them; officials able to recite them were classed with the Xiaojing.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Under Emperor Ping, Wang Mang wrote eight moral essays for his heirs and had the Imperial Academy teach them; clerks who memorized them were ranked like students of the Xiaojing.〉

sentences.zh: 音義云：「言用之得選舉之也。」〉

translations.literal: The Yin yi says: 'It means using this to obtain recommendation.'〉
translations.idiomatic: A phonological commentary explains that mastering those texts helped one win nomination.〉

sentences.zh: 夫喪親自盡，孝之終也。

translations.literal: Mourning one's parents to the utmost is the end of filial piety.
translations.idiomatic: To exhaust one's grief for parents is the culmination of filial duty.

sentences.zh: 〈盡謂盡其哀戚也。〉

translations.literal: 〈'Exhaust' means exhausting grief and sorrow.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈"Exhaust" here means giving full expression to grief.〉

sentences.zh: 今之公卿及二千石，三年之喪，不得即去，殆非所以增崇孝道而克稱火德者也。

translations.literal: Now the ministers and those at the two-thousand-shi rank cannot leave office immediately for three years' mourning—this is hardly the way to elevate filial teaching and match the virtue of Fire.
translations.idiomatic: Yet today high ministers and officials paid two thousand shi cannot leave their posts to observe three years' mourning; that can scarcely exalt filial teaching or live up to the Fire virtue the Han claims.

sentences.zh: 往者孝文勞謙，行過乎儉，

translations.literal: Formerly Emperor Xiaowen labored at modesty; his conduct exceeded in austerity.
translations.idiomatic: Formerly Emperor Wen practiced strenuous humility; his personal austerity overshot the mean.

sentences.zh: 〈《易》謙卦九三爻：「勞謙君子，有終吉。」〉

translations.literal: 〈The Yi, Qian hexagram, third line: 'The modest gentleman who toils—there is a good end.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Book of Changes, Qian's third line: "The humble gentleman who keeps toiling wins a fortunate outcome."〉

sentences.zh: 故有遺詔以日易月。

translations.literal: Therefore there was a testamentary edict substituting days for months.
translations.idiomatic: Hence his final edict allowed officials to abbreviate mourning—counting days as months.

sentences.zh: 此當時之宜，不可貫之萬世。

translations.literal: This was appropriate for that time; it cannot be threaded through ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: That compromise suited his age; it cannot be codified for all time.

sentences.zh: 古今之制雖有損益，而諒闇之禮未嘗改移，以示天下莫遺其親。

translations.literal: Though ancient and modern regulations have waxed and waned, the rites of bereavement seclusion have never been altered, to show the realm not to forget their parents.
translations.idiomatic: Rites have changed across dynasties, but the core mourning seclusion has endured—to remind the world never to neglect one's parents.

sentences.zh: 〈遺，忘也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Yi means 'forget.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Here yi carries the sense of to forget or neglect.〉

sentences.zh: 今公卿羣寮皆政教所瞻，而父母之喪不得奔赴。

translations.literal: Now the ministers and hosts of officials are what governance and instruction look to, yet for parents' funerals they cannot rush to attend.
translations.idiomatic: The very men who exemplify statecraft are barred from rushing home when parents die.

sentences.zh: 夫仁義之行，自上而始；

translations.literal: The practice of benevolence and right begins from above;
translations.idiomatic: Humane and righteous conduct must begin with those on high;

sentences.zh: 敦厚之俗，以應乎下。

translations.literal: the custom of honest generosity responds below.
translations.idiomatic: only then can honest, generous customs take root among the people.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「喪祭之禮闕，則人臣之恩薄，背死忘生者眾矣。

translations.literal: The tradition says: 'If rites of mourning and sacrifice are deficient, the kindness of subjects grows thin, and many turn their backs on the dead and forget the living.'
translations.idiomatic: The classical texts warn that when funeral and sacrificial rites lapse, the bond between ruler and minister weakens, and many abandon the dead to court the living.

sentences.zh: 」曾子曰：「人未有自致者，必也親喪乎！」

translations.literal: ' Master Zeng said: 'Among men there is none who brings it forth of himself; it must be in mourning for parents!''
translations.idiomatic: Master Zeng added, "No one summons that depth of feeling on purpose—it breaks forth only in the loss of a parent."'

sentences.zh: 〈事見《論語》。

translations.literal: 〈The matter is seen in the Lunyu.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The passage appears in the Analects.'〉

sentences.zh: 致猶盡也，極也。〉

translations.literal: Zhi means 'exhaust,' 'extremity.'〉
translations.idiomatic: "Bring forth" here means to exhaust one's feeling utterly.〉

sentences.zh: 春秋傳曰：「上之所為，民之歸也。」

translations.literal: The Chunqiu zhuan says: 'What those above do is where the people turn.'
translations.idiomatic: The commentaries say, "The people follow wherever their superiors lead."

sentences.zh: 〈《左氏傳》臧武仲之言。〉

translations.literal: 〈Words of Zang Wuzhong in the Zuoshi zhuan.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈This comes from Zang Wuzhong in the Zuo Tradition.〉

sentences.zh: 夫上所不為而民或為之，故加刑罰；

translations.literal: What those above do not do yet the people sometimes do—therefore apply punishments;
translations.idiomatic: When commoners do what their betters forbid, the law punishes them;

sentences.zh: 若上之所為，民亦為之，又何誅焉？

translations.literal: if what those above do the people also do, why execute them?
translations.idiomatic: but when ruler and people share the same fault, blame cannot rest on the people alone.

sentences.zh: 昔丞相翟方進，以自備宰相，而不敢踰制。

translations.literal: Formerly Chancellor Zhai Fangjin, because he held himself as chief minister, did not dare transgress the regulations.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Zhai Fangjin once held himself to the highest standard and would not bend the rules even for himself.

sentences.zh: 至遭母憂，三十六日而除。

translations.literal: When it came to mourning for his mother, after thirty-six days he ended it.
translations.idiomatic: Yet when his mother died he observed only thirty-six days of mourning before returning to duty.

sentences.zh: 〈前書翟方進為丞相，遭後母憂，行服三十六日起視事，曰：「不敢踰國制也。」〉

translations.literal: 〈The earlier history: Zhai Fangjin as chancellor, encountering mourning for his stepmother, observed mourning thirty-six days then handled affairs, saying: 'I dare not transgress the state's regulations.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Han shu relates that as chancellor he mourned a stepmother for thirty-six days, then resumed office saying he dared not exceed national precedent.〉

sentences.zh: 夫失禮之源，自上而始。

translations.literal: The source of losing ritual begins from above.
translations.idiomatic: When ritual collapses, the rot starts at the top.

sentences.zh: 古者大喪三年不呼其門，

translations.literal: In antiquity for great mourning three years they did not call at his gate,
translations.idiomatic: Antiquity demanded three years of mourning during which no state business intruded at one's gate,

sentences.zh: 〈《公羊傳》之文也。

translations.literal: 〈This is text from the Gongyang zhuan.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈As the Gongyang commentary puts it.'〉

sentences.zh: 何休注云：「重奪孝子之恩。」〉

translations.literal: He Xiu's commentary says: 'It heavily removes the filial son's favor.'〉
translations.idiomatic: He Xiu explains that this rule shields the mourner's grief from intrusion.〉

sentences.zh: 所以崇國厚俗篤化之道也。

translations.literal: This is the way to honor the state, thicken custom, and deepen transformation.
translations.idiomatic: That is how a state honors grief, thickens honest customs, and deepens moral transformation.

sentences.zh: 事失宜正。

translations.literal: When affairs miss the mark, they should be corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Errors in policy ought to be righted.

sentences.zh: 過勿憚改。

translations.literal: In faults, do not shrink from reform.
translations.idiomatic: When wrong, do not fear to change course.

sentences.zh: 〈憚，難也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Dan means 'difficulty.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Dan here means to shrink from or dread.〉

sentences.zh: 天下通喪，可如舊禮。

translations.literal: For universal mourning throughout the realm, one may follow the old rites.
translations.idiomatic: Let the empire's universal mourning again follow the classical three-year standard.

sentences.zh: 〈《禮記》曰：「三年之喪，天下之通喪也。」〉

translations.literal: 〈The Liji says: 'Three years' mourning is the universal mourning of the realm.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Book of Rites declares that three years of mourning is the mourning obligation shared by the whole world.〉

sentences.zh: 臣聞有夫婦然後有父子，有父子然後有君臣，有君臣然後有上下，有上下然後有禮義。

translations.literal: I have heard that there are husband and wife, and then there are father and son; there are father and son, and then there are ruler and minister; there are ruler and minister, and then there are high and low; there are high and low, and then there are ritual and moral rightness.
translations.idiomatic: I was taught that marriage begets the family, the family begets the state, ranks arise from ruler and minister, and only then can ritual and righteousness take shape.

sentences.zh: 禮義備，則人知所厝矣。

translations.literal: When ritual and moral rightness are complete, then people know where to stand.
translations.idiomatic: Once ritual and right conduct are in place, people know how to order their lives.

sentences.zh: 〈語見易序卦也。〉

translations.literal: 〈The wording appears in the Xu gua [sequence of hexagrams] of the Changes.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈This idea appears in the "Sequence of the Hexagrams" in the Zhouyi.〉

sentences.zh: 夫婦人倫之始，王化之端，故文王作易，上經首干、坤，下經首鹹、恆。

translations.literal: Husband and wife are the beginning of human relationships and the starting point of royal transformation; therefore King Wen composed the Changes: the upper classic begins with Qian and Kun; the lower classic begins with Xian and Heng.
translations.idiomatic: Marriage is the first bond in human ethics and the seed of civilizing rule; hence King Wen arranged the Zhouyi so the upper wing opens with Qian and Kun and the lower wing with Xian and Heng.

sentences.zh: 〈易乾、坤至離為上經，鹹、恆至未濟為下經。〉

translations.literal: 〈From Qian and Kun through Li forms the upper classic; from Xian and Heng through Weiji forms the lower classic.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The upper cycle runs from Qian and Kun to Li; the lower cycle from Xian and Heng to Weiji.〉

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「天尊地卑，乾坤定矣。」

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'Heaven is exalted and earth is humble; thus Qian and Kun are fixed.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said, "Heaven ranks above and earth below—thereby the hexagrams Qian and Kun establish the cosmic pattern."

sentences.zh: 〈易系辭也。〉

translations.literal: 〈From the Xi ci zhuan of the Changes.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈This is quoted from the "Appended Remarks" to the Zhouyi.〉

sentences.zh: 夫婦之道，所謂順也。

translations.literal: The way of husband and wife is what is called compliant obedience.
translations.idiomatic: Conjugal relations are meant to exemplify harmony and deference.

sentences.zh: 堯典曰：「厘降二女於媯汭，嬪於虞。

translations.literal: The Canon of Yao says: 'He solemnly sent down his two daughters to the bend of the Gui, as wives in Yu's household.
translations.idiomatic: The Canon of Yao records that he gave his two daughters in marriage at the bend of the Gui River to become wives in Yu's house.

sentences.zh: 」降者下也，嬪者婦也。

translations.literal: 'Jiang' means to descend; 'pin' means wife.
translations.idiomatic: The word "sent down" signals humility; "wives" denotes their role as spouses.

sentences.zh: 言雖帝堯之女，下嫁於虞，猶屈體降下，勤修婦道。

translations.literal: This means that although they were Emperor Yao's daughters, marrying down to Yu, they still humbled their persons and diligently cultivated the way of the wife.
translations.idiomatic: Although they were the emperor's daughters marrying beneath their station to Yu, they still humbled themselves and devoted themselves to proper wifely conduct.

sentences.zh: 易曰：「帝乙歸妹，以祉元吉。」

translations.literal: The Changes says: 'Di Yi married off his younger sister; there is blessing and supreme good fortune.'
translations.idiomatic: The Zhouyi says, "King Di Yi gave his sister in marriage—great fortune and the highest good."

sentences.zh: 〈易泰卦六五爻辭也。

translations.literal: 〈This is the Fifth Yin line's oracle text in the hexagram Tai from the Changes.
translations.idiomatic: 〈This gloss cites the upper trigram's fifth line of the hexagram Tai.〉

sentences.zh: 王輔嗣注云：「婦人謂嫁曰歸。

translations.literal: Wang Bi's commentary says: 'For women, marrying out is called returning.'
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bi glosses the line: for a woman, going to her husband's house is "returning."

sentences.zh: 泰者，陰陽交通之時，女處尊位，履中居順，降身應二，帝乙歸妹，誠合斯義也。

translations.literal: Tai is the time when yin and yang interact; the woman occupies the honored position, treads the center and dwells in compliance, lowers herself to respond to the second line—Di Yi marrying off his younger sister truly accords with this meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Under Tai, yin and yang meet in balance; the bride occupies a dignified place yet stays centered and yielding, answering the second line—so Di Yi's marriage alliance embodies the image.

sentences.zh: 」案《史記》紂父名帝乙，此文以帝乙為湯，湯名天乙也。〉

translations.literal: ' According to the Shiji, Zhou's father was named Di Yi; this passage identifies Di Yi with Tang; Tang's name was Tian Yi.'〉
translations.idiomatic: The Records treat Zhou's father as Di Yi, while this passage equates Di Yi with Tang (whose formal name was Tian Yi).〉

sentences.zh: 婦人謂嫁曰歸，言湯以娶禮歸其妹於諸侯也。

translations.literal: For women, marrying out is called 'returning'; this says Tang used the wedding rites to marry his younger sister to the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: Calling marriage "returning" shows Tang escorting his sister to her husband among the regional lords with full nuptial ceremony.

sentences.zh: 春秋之義，王姬嫁齊，使魯主之，不以天子之尊加於諸侯也。

translations.literal: The principle of the Chunqiu: when the king's daughter married into Qi, Lu was made host, so as not to impose the Son of Heaven's dignity upon the feudal lords.
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn principle is illustrated when the Zhou princess wedded into Qi and Lu acted as master of ceremonies—keeping the Son of Heaven from placing his majesty directly over the nobles.

sentences.zh: 〈《公羊傳》曰：「夏單伯逆王姬。

translations.literal: 〈The Gongyang zhuan says: 'In summer, Shan Bo welcomed the king's daughter.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Gongyang commentary records: "In summer Duke Shan went to meet the royal bride."

sentences.zh: 單伯者何？

translations.literal: Who is Shan Bo?
translations.idiomatic: Who was Duke Shan?

sentences.zh: 吾大夫之命於天子者。

translations.literal: Our grandee commissioned by the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: A Lu nobleman holding a royal commission.

sentences.zh: 何以不稱使？

translations.literal: Why is he not called 'envoy'?
translations.idiomatic: Why is he not styled as an envoy?

sentences.zh: 天子召而使逆之。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven summoned him and had him welcome her.
translations.idiomatic: The king summoned him personally to perform the meeting ceremony.

sentences.zh: 逆之者何？

translations.literal: What does welcoming her mean?
translations.idiomatic: What does "meet her" imply?

sentences.zh: 使我主之也。

translations.literal: That we should host her.
translations.idiomatic: That Lu would receive her as host.

sentences.zh: 曷為使我主之？

translations.literal: Why have us host her?
translations.idiomatic: Why should Lu play host?

sentences.zh: 天子嫁女於諸侯，必使同姓諸侯主之。

translations.literal: When the Son of Heaven marries a daughter to a feudal lord, he always has a lord of the same surname act as host.
translations.idiomatic: When the Zhou king married a daughter to a vassal, a ruler of the same clan always stood as host—never the bride's father himself.

sentences.zh: 」何休注云：「不自為主，尊卑不敵也。」〉

translations.literal: ' He Xiu's commentary: 'He does not host himself—honored and lowly are not peers.'〉
translations.idiomatic: He Xiu explains that the king cannot receive the groom himself without violating the hierarchy between supreme lord and vassal.〉

sentences.zh: 今漢承秦法，設尚主之儀，以妻制夫，以卑臨尊，違乾坤之道，失陽唱之義。

translations.literal: Now Han inherits Qin's laws and institutes the rite of honoring the imperial daughter as wife—wife controlling husband, the humble domineering over the honored—violating the way of Qian and Kun and losing the meaning of yang taking the lead.
translations.idiomatic: The Han has kept Qin-era precedent that elevates an imperial princess above her husband—wives commanding husbands and inferiors lording over superiors—running counter to Qian and Kun and to the norm that yang leads and yin follows.

sentences.zh: 〈易緯曰「陽唱而陰和」也。〉

translations.literal: 〈The Yi wei says: 'Yang leads and yin harmonizes.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈An apocryphon on the Changes states that yang initiates while yin responds.〉

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「昔聖人之作易也，仰則觀象於天，俯則察法於池，鶯鳥獸之文，與地之宜。

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'When the sages of old made the Changes, they looked up to observe images in heaven, looked down to inspect patterns on the earth, and contemplated the patterns of birds and beasts and the suitability of the land.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said the ancient sages composed the Changes by reading celestial images above, terrestrial patterns below, and the markings of birds and beasts together with how each region nourishes life.

sentences.zh: 近取諸身，遠取諸物，以通神明之德，以類萬物之情。」

translations.literal: They drew near from their own persons and far from things, thereby to penetrate the virtue of spirit luminaries and classify the dispositions of the myriad things.'
translations.idiomatic: They took images from their own bodies and from every kind of thing, linking numinous virtue with the myriad beings and their inclinations."

sentences.zh: 〈皆易系之文也。〉

translations.literal: 〈All are texts from the Xi ci zhuan of the Changes.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈These lines come from the "Appended Remarks."〉

sentences.zh: 今觀法於天，則北極至尊，四星妃後。

translations.literal: Now observing patterns in heaven, the Pole Star is supremely honored; four stars mark empress and consort.
translations.idiomatic: Look at the sky: the Pole Star sits supreme while surrounding stars mark the queen and concubines.

sentences.zh: 〈北極，北辰也。

translations.literal: 〈The Pole Star is the northern asterism.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The apex star is the Pole Star.

sentences.zh: 軒轅四星，女主之象也。〉

translations.literal: The four stars of Xuanyuan are the image of the female ruler.'〉
translations.idiomatic: The Xuanyuan asterism's four stars symbolize the mistress of the inner palace.〉

sentences.zh: 察法於地，則櫫山象夫，卑澤象妻。

translations.literal: Inspecting patterns on earth, lofty hills image the husband and lowly marshes image the wife.
translations.idiomatic: On earth, mountains rise like husbands and marshes lie low like wives.

sentences.zh: 〈櫫猶高也。

translations.literal: 〈Ju means high.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Here ju means "lofty.".

sentences.zh: 易艮下兌上為鹹。

translations.literal: In the Changes, Gen below and Dui above form Xian.
translations.idiomatic: The hexagram Xian stacks Gen beneath Dui.

sentences.zh: 艮為山，夫象也。

translations.literal: Gen is mountain—the image of the husband.
translations.idiomatic: Gen represents the mountain—emblem of the husband.

sentences.zh: 兌為澤，妻象也。

translations.literal: Dui is marsh—the image of the wife.
translations.idiomatic: Dui represents the marsh—emblem of the wife.

sentences.zh: 鹹，感也。

translations.literal: Xian means stimulus and resonance.
translations.idiomatic: The name Xian denotes mutual resonance.

sentences.zh: 山澤通氣，夫婦之相感也。〉

translations.literal: Mountain and marsh communicate qi—this is husband and wife resonating with each other.'〉
translations.idiomatic: Where mountain and marsh exchange vapor, husband and wife interact—such is the image of Xian.〉

sentences.zh: 鶯鳥獸之文，鳥則雄者鳴鴝，雌能順服；

translations.literal: Among the patterns of birds and beasts: among birds the male calls and pipes, the female complies;
translations.idiomatic: Among birds the cock crows and pipes while the hen answers in submission;

sentences.zh: 獸則牡為唱導，牝乃相從。

translations.literal: among beasts the male leads and calls, the female follows.
translations.idiomatic: among mammals the stag leads and the doe follows.

sentences.zh: 近取諸身，則干為人首，坤為人腹。

translations.literal: Drawing near from the person, Qian is the human head and Kun is the belly.
translations.idiomatic: Taking the human body as metaphor, Qian is the head and Kun the belly.

sentences.zh: 〈易說卦之文也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Text from the Shuogua of the Changes.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈As the "Explanation of the Trigrams" teaches.〉

sentences.zh: 遠取諸物，則木實屬天，根荄屬地。

translations.literal: Drawing far from things, tree fruit belongs to heaven and roots and tubers belong to earth.
translations.idiomatic: Among things, fruit aloft belongs to heaven while roots below belong to earth.

sentences.zh: 〈荄音該。〉

translations.literal: 〈Gai is read gai.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The character gai is pronounced like the syllable gai.〉

sentences.zh: 陽尊陰卑，蓋乃天性。

translations.literal: Yang is honored and yin is lowly—such is surely heaven's nature.
translations.idiomatic: Superior yang and subordinate yin reflect the order written into nature.

sentences.zh: 且詩初篇實首關雎；

translations.literal: Moreover the first poem of the Shi truly begins with 'Guan guan cry the ospreys';
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Songs opens with the "Ospreys"—marriage as moral foundation;

sentences.zh: 禮始冠、婚，先正夫婦。

translations.literal: the Li begins with capping and wedding—first rectifying husband and wife.
translations.idiomatic: the ritual canon begins with capping and wedding because spouses must be set right first.

sentences.zh: 〈儀禮士冠禮為始，士婚禮次之。〉

translations.literal: 〈The Yili takes the Scholar's Capping Rite as beginning and the Scholar's Wedding Rite next.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Etiquette and Rites starts with the commoner's capping ceremony and follows with his nuptials.〉

sentences.zh: 天地六經，其旨一揆。

translations.literal: Heaven and earth and the six classics—their purport is one measure.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven, earth, and the six classics share a single intent.

sentences.zh: 宜改尚主之制，以稱乾坤之性。

translations.literal: It is fitting to reform the institution of honoring the princess as wife, to match the nature of Qian and Kun.
translations.idiomatic: The court should revise the institution that subordinates husbands to imperial brides so that policy once again accords with Qian and Kun.

sentences.zh: 遵法堯、湯，式是周、孔。

translations.literal: Follow the models of Yao and Tang; take the Duke of Zhou and Confucius as pattern.
translations.idiomatic: Take Yao and Tang for historical precedent and the Duke of Zhou and Confucius for ritual authority.

sentences.zh: 〈式，法也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Shi means model.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Shi means "to take as model."〉

sentences.zh: 合之天地而不謬，質之鬼神而不疑。

translations.literal: Joined with heaven and earth it is not in error; tested by ghosts and spirits it raises no doubt.
translations.idiomatic: So aligned with heaven and earth it cannot be wrong; laid before spirits it admits no doubt.

sentences.zh: 人事如此，則嘉瑞降天，吉符出地，五韙鹹備，各以其敍矣。

translations.literal: If human affairs are thus, then auspicious omens descend from heaven and lucky signs emerge from earth; the five confirmations are all prepared, each in its proper order.
translations.idiomatic: Human conduct shaped like this draws favorable omens from above and propitious signs from below—the five auspicious responses arrive, each in its season.

sentences.zh: 〈韙，是也。

translations.literal: 〈Wei means 'this is correct.'
translations.idiomatic: 〈Wei glosses as "right" or "verified.".

sentences.zh: 《史記》曰：「休征：曰肅，時雨若；

translations.literal: The Shiji says: 'The favorable correspondences: reverence—then timely rains accord;
translations.idiomatic: The Historical Records enumerate auspicious responses from the "Great Plan": when solemn reverence prevails, rains fall in season;

sentences.zh: 曰乂，時

translations.literal: called orderly rule—timely
translations.idiomatic: when orderly administration prevails, timely

translations.literal: (yang)
translations.idiomatic: (interlinear note: read the following graph as yang 'sunshine')

sentences.zh: [暘]若；

translations.literal: [yang] accords;
translations.idiomatic: sunshine agrees with the season;

sentences.zh: 曰哲，時燠若；

translations.literal: called wisdom—timely warmth accords;
translations.idiomatic: when perspicacity prevails, heat stays temperate;

sentences.zh: 曰謀，時寒若；

translations.literal: called counsel—timely cold accords;
translations.idiomatic: when deliberation prevails, cold arrives as it should;

sentences.zh: 曰聖，時風若。

translations.literal: called sagacity—timely winds accord.
translations.idiomatic: when sagacity prevails, winds blow seasonably.

sentences.zh: 」五是來備，各以其敍也。〉

translations.literal: ' When these five are all present, each follows its proper sequence.'〉
translations.idiomatic: When all five responses appear in order, harmony is complete throughout heaven and earth.〉

sentences.zh: 昔者聖人建天地之中而謂之禮，禮者，所以興福祥之本，而止禍亂之源也。

translations.literal: Formerly the sages established the center between heaven and earth and called it ritual; ritual is that by which to raise the root of blessing and fortune and stop the source of disaster and disorder.
translations.idiomatic: The sages framed ritual as the pivot between heaven and earth—the taproot of blessing and the dam against chaos.

sentences.zh: 人能枉欲從禮者，則福歸之；

translations.literal: When people can bend desire to follow ritual, fortune returns to them;
translations.idiomatic: Those who curb appetite with ritual harvest good fortune;

sentences.zh: 順情廢禮者，則禍歸之。

translations.literal: when they indulge feeling and abandon ritual, disaster returns to them.
translations.idiomatic: those who pamper passion and discard ritual invite calamity.

sentences.zh: 推禍福之所應，知興廢之所由來也。

translations.literal: Inferring how fortune and disaster respond, one knows whence rise and fall come.
translations.idiomatic: Tracing how blessings and curses follow conduct reveals why dynasties flourish or fail.

sentences.zh: 眾禮之中，婚禮為首。

translations.literal: Among the many rites, the wedding rite comes first.
translations.idiomatic: Among all ceremonies, marriage ranks first.

sentences.zh: 故天子娶十二，天之數也；

translations.literal: Therefore the Son of Heaven takes twelve wives—it is heaven's number;
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven takes twelve consorts—matching heaven's dozen months;

sentences.zh: 諸侯以下各有等差，事之降也。

translations.literal: feudal lords downward each have graded differences—affairs descend accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: nobles below step down by rank because human obligations mirror cosmic gradation.

sentences.zh: 〈《白武通》曰：「天子娶十二，法天，則有十二月，百物畢生也。

translations.literal: 〈The Baihu tong says: 'The Son of Heaven takes twelve wives, modeling heaven—there are twelve months; the hundred things all come to life.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Baihu tong explains that twelve royal consorts mirror twelve months and the yearly rebirth of creatures.

sentences.zh: 」又曰「諸侯娶九女」也。〉

translations.literal: ' It also says 'feudal lords take nine wives.'〉
translations.idiomatic: The same text limits feudal lords to nine wives.〉

sentences.zh: 陽性純而能施，陰體順而能化，以禮濟樂，節宣其氣。

translations.literal: Yang's nature is pure and can bestow; yin's substance is compliant and can transform; ritual completes music and moderates and vents their qi.
translations.idiomatic: Yang is pure and generative, yin yielding and transformative; ritual balances music and regulates vital energies.

sentences.zh: 〈《左傳》曰，昔晉侯有疾，醫和視之，曰：「疾不可為也。

translations.literal: 〈The Zuozhuan says: Formerly the Marquis of Jin was ill; Physician He examined him and said: 'The illness cannot be cured.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Zuo Tradition tells how Physician He told the ailing Marquis of Jin,

sentences.zh: 是為近女室，疾如蠱，非鬼非食，惑以喪志。

translations.literal: This is from closeness to women's chambers; the illness is like gu—neither ghost nor food; delusion has lost the mind.'
translations.idiomatic: "Your sickness comes from the women's quarters—it mimics gu poison; neither ghost nor diet explains it; excess has stolen your will."

sentences.zh: 」公曰：「女不可近乎？

translations.literal: ' The duke said: 'Must one not draw near to women?'
translations.idiomatic: The marquis asked whether men must shun women entirely.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「節之。

translations.literal: ' He replied: 'Moderate it.
translations.idiomatic: The physician answered: "Temper it."

sentences.zh: 先王之樂，所以節百事也。

translations.literal: The music of the former kings is what moderates the hundred affairs.'
translations.idiomatic: The kings used ritual music to keep every affair within bounds."

sentences.zh: 天有六氣，過則為災。

translations.literal: Heaven has six qi; excess becomes disaster.'
translations.idiomatic: Heaven sends six climates; intemperance turns them into scourges."

sentences.zh: 」於是乎節宣其氣也。〉

translations.literal: ' Therefore one harmonizes and vents that qi.'〉
translations.idiomatic: That is why the former kings used ritual and music to temper those vital forces.〉

sentences.zh: 故能豐子孫之祥，致老壽之福。

translations.literal: Thus one can enrich the auspiciousness of descendants and bring the blessing of long life.
translations.idiomatic: Balance yields prolific, fortunate heirs and the boon of longevity.

sentences.zh: 及三代之季，淫而無節。

translations.literal: By the late Three Dynasties there was debauchery without restraint.
translations.idiomatic: Yet by the late Xia, Shang, and Zhou, lust knew no limit.

sentences.zh: 瑤台、傾宮，陳妾數百。

translations.literal: Jade terraces and tilting palaces—hundreds of concubines ranked.
translations.idiomatic: Jade towers and leaning palaces housed hundreds of stacked concubines.

sentences.zh: 〈列女傳曰，夏桀為琁室、瑤台，以臨雲雨，紂為傾宮。

translations.literal: 〈The Biographies of Women says: Jie of Xia made the Xuan chamber and jade terrace to overlook cloud and rain; Zhou made the tilting palace.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Liu Xiang's Biographies of Women describes Jie's ringed chamber and jade terrace reaching into the clouds and Zhou's leaning palace.

sentences.zh: 解見桓帝紀也。〉

translations.literal: Explanation seen in Emperor Huan's annals.'〉
translations.idiomatic: Details appear in the annals of Emperor Huan.〉

sentences.zh: 陽竭於上，陰隔於下。

translations.literal: Yang was exhausted above; yin was blocked below.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven's yang burned out aloft while yin stagnated below.

sentences.zh: 故周公之戒曰：「不知稼穡之艱難，不聞小人之勞，惟耽樂之從，時亦罔或克壽。

translations.literal: Therefore the Duke of Zhou's warning says: 'Not knowing the hardship of sowing and reaping, not hearing the toil of petty men, only following indulgent pleasure—none in such times could attain long life.'
translations.idiomatic: So the Duke of Zhou warns: ignoring farmers' toil, deaf to common labor, drowning in pleasure—such rulers never grow old.

sentences.zh: 」是其明戒。

translations.literal: This is its clear warning.
translations.idiomatic: That passage is the plain admonition.

sentences.zh: 〈事見《尚書無逸篇》，其詞與此微有不同也。〉

translations.literal: 〈The matter is in the 'Wu yi' chapter of the Shang shu; the wording differs slightly from this.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The story appears in the "Against Luxurious Ease" chapter of the Documents, with wording slightly different.〉

sentences.zh: 後世之人，好福不務其本，惡禍不易其軌。

translations.literal: People of later ages love fortune but do not work at its root; hate disaster but do not change their course.
translations.idiomatic: Later generations crave blessings yet refuse to cultivate them; dread ruin yet refuse to mend their ways.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「𢧵趾適履，孰雲其愚？

translations.literal: The tradition says: 'Cutting the toes to fit the shoe—who calls that foolish?
translations.idiomatic: A proverb asks who would call it foolish to chop one's toes to fit a shoe—

sentences.zh: 何與斯人，追欲喪軀？

translations.literal: Why join such people, chasing desire to ruin the body?
translations.idiomatic: yet many march with that crowd, chasing lust until their bodies collapse.

sentences.zh: 」誠可痛也。

translations.literal: ' Truly one must grieve at that.'
translations.idiomatic: That finale lands as bitter indictment rather than mere folly.

sentences.zh: 〈適猶從也。

translations.literal: 〈Shi means 'accord with' or 'follow.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Here shi glosses as "to follow" or "to comply with."〉

sentences.zh: 言喪身之愚，甚於□趾也。〉

translations.literal: It says the folly of ruining one's life is worse than [mutilating] the toes.〉
translations.idiomatic: The gloss adds that self-destruction through lust outstrips the folly of cutting one's feet to fit a shoe.〉

sentences.zh: 臣竊聞後宮采女五六千人，從官侍使復在其外。

translations.literal: I have privately heard that the inner palace has five or six thousand selected women, and attendants and serving staff are beyond that number.
translations.idiomatic: I have heard in private that the harem numbers five or six thousand ladies-in-waiting, with eunuch attendants and staff in addition.

sentences.zh: 冬夏衣服，朝夕稟糧，耗費縑帛，空竭府藏，徵調增倍，十而稅一，空賦不辜之民，以供無用之女，百姓窮困於外，陰陽隔塞於內。

translations.literal: Winter and summer clothing, morning and evening rations, exhaust silk and deplete the treasuries; impositions and levies are doubled, and a tithe of one in ten is taken; emptying the blameless people with taxes to supply useless women—the commoners are destitute without, yin and yang are blocked within.
translations.idiomatic: They need clothes for every season, food at every turn, and the cost in silk and grain empties the state coffers. Levies are doubled to a one-in-ten exaction, squeezing innocent subjects to keep idle concubines—the people are beggared in the open while harmony between yin and yang is stifled at court.

sentences.zh: 故感動和氣，災異屢臻。

translations.literal: Therefore the harmonious qi is disturbed, and disaster and prodigy arrive repeatedly.
translations.idiomatic: No wonder the vital harmony is jarred and portents keep appearing.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為諸非禮聘未曾幸御者，一皆遣出，使成妃合。

translations.literal: I, foolish, think that all who were not taken in proper betrothal rites and have never been favored by the sovereign should in every case be sent out, to let them form proper wedlock.
translations.idiomatic: I humbly suggest that every woman not lawfully betrothed and never visited by the throne be released to marry in due form.

sentences.zh: 一曰通怨曠，和陰陽。

translations.literal: First, to open the way for the widowed and unmarried, and harmonize yin and yang.
translations.idiomatic: First, it would relieve men and women trapped outside marriage and restore balance between yin and yang.

sentences.zh: 二曰省財用，實府藏。

translations.literal: Second, to save expenditure and fill the treasuries.
translations.idiomatic: Second, it would curb spending and replenish the state's reserves.

sentences.zh: 三曰修禮制，綏眉壽。

translations.literal: Third, to cultivate ritual norms and secure long life.
translations.idiomatic: Third, it would uphold ceremonial standards and foster longevity.

sentences.zh: 四曰配陽施，祈螽斯。

translations.literal: Fourth, to match the yang bestowal [of the sovereign] and pray for teeming offspring.
translations.idiomatic: Fourth, it would align the emperor's procreative role with Heaven and pray for abundant heirs.

sentences.zh: 〈螽斯，蚣蝑也，其性不妒，故能子孫眾多。

translations.literal: 〈The zhongsi are katydids; by nature they are not jealous, hence they can have numerous descendants.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The ode's zhongsi bugs are long-horned grasshoppers, proverbially free of jealousy, hence a metaphor for prolific families.

sentences.zh: 詩曰：「螽斯羽，詵詵兮。

translations.literal: The Shi says: 'The wings of the zhongsi, thickly clustered;
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Songs sings, "Thick flutter the zhongsi's wings;"

sentences.zh: 宜爾子孫，振振兮。」〉

translations.literal: may your sons and grandsons be numerous and thriving.'〉
translations.idiomatic: may your descendants multiply in thriving hosts."〉

sentences.zh: 五曰寬役賦，安黎民。

translations.literal: Fifth, to lighten labor and taxes and settle the black-haired people.
translations.idiomatic: Fifth, it would ease corvée and grain levies and bring peace to the common people.

sentences.zh: 此誠國家之弘利，天人之大福也。

translations.literal: This is truly the realm's great profit and Heaven and man's supreme blessing.
translations.idiomatic: That would serve both statecraft and cosmic blessing at once.

sentences.zh: 夫寒熱晦明，所以為歲；

translations.literal: Cold and heat, dark and light—these make the year;
translations.idiomatic: Cold and heat, night and day—these turns mark the year;

sentences.zh: 尊卑奢儉，所以為禮：故以晦明寒暑之氣，尊卑侈約之禮為其節也。

translations.literal: high and low, lavish and austere—these make ritual: therefore one takes the qi of dark and bright, cold and heat, and the ritual of high and low, extravagance and restraint, as its moderating knots.
translations.idiomatic: rank and sumptuary scale—these define ritual. Seasonal rhythm and hierarchical decorum must therefore set the limits.

sentences.zh: 易曰：「天地節而四時成。」

translations.literal: The Changes says: 'Heaven and earth have moderation, and the four seasons are completed.'
translations.idiomatic: The Zhouyi says, "When heaven and earth observe limits, the four seasons complete their round."

sentences.zh: 〈節卦彖辭文也。〉

translations.literal: 〈This is the text of the Judgment on the hexagram Jie.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈These words belong to the hexagram Jie's Judgment.〉

sentences.zh: 春秋傳曰：「唯器與名不可以假人。」

translations.literal: The Chunqiu zhuan says: 'Only vessels and names may not be lent to others.'
translations.idiomatic: The Spring and Autumn commentaries warn that regalia and titles must never be delegated away.

sentences.zh: 〈杜預注左氏云：「器謂車服，名謂爵號。」〉

translations.literal: 〈Du Yu annotating the Zuoshi zhuan says: 'Vessels means chariots and robes; names means ranks and titles.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Du Yu glosses "implements" as chariots and insignia and "names" as noble ranks.〉

sentences.zh: 《孝經》曰：「安上治民，莫善於禮。

translations.literal: The Xiaojing says: 'To settle superiors and govern the people, nothing is better than ritual.
translations.idiomatic: The Classic of Filial Piety teaches that nothing settles rulers and orders the people like ritual—

sentences.zh: 」禮者，尊卑之差，上下之制也。

translations.literal: ' Ritual is the gradation of honored and humble, the regulation of high and low.
translations.idiomatic: for ritual encodes who ranks above whom and how far authority extends.

sentences.zh: 昔季氏八佾舞於庭，非有傷害困於人物，而孔子猶曰「是可忍也，孰不可忍」。

translations.literal: Formerly the Ji clan danced the eight rows in their courtyard—there was no harm or injury to persons—yet Confucius still said, 'If this can be borne, what cannot be borne?'
translations.idiomatic: When the Ji clan staged the eight-row dance at home, no one was physically injured, yet Confucius cried, "If we tolerate this, what will we not tolerate?"

sentences.zh: 洪範曰：「惟闢作威，惟闢作福，惟辟玉食。

translations.literal: The Hong Fan says: 'Only the sovereign may wield awe; only the sovereign may dispense blessings; only the sovereign enjoys jade vessels at meals.
translations.idiomatic: The Great Plan warns that awe-inspiring punishments, blessings, and feasting from jade vessels belong to the ruler alone—

sentences.zh: 」凡此三者，君所獨行而臣不得同也。

translations.literal: ' All three are solely the ruler's prerogative; ministers may not share them.
translations.idiomatic: These three prerogatives belong to the sovereign alone; subjects must never share in them.

sentences.zh: 今臣僭君服，下食上珍，所謂害於而家，凶於而國者也。

translations.literal: Now ministers usurp the ruler's dress, and inferiors consume what superiors prize—this is what is called harm to your house and calamity to your state.
translations.idiomatic: Yet subjects now wear what belongs on the throne and dine like emperors—the very harm to household and realm the classics dread.

sentences.zh: 宜略依古禮尊卑之差，及董仲舒制度之別，

translations.literal: It is fitting broadly to follow the ancient ritual distinctions between honored and humble, together with Dong Zhongshu's graded institutions,
translations.idiomatic: Policy should restore ancient distinctions of rank and Dong Zhongshu's sumptuary scheme—

sentences.zh: 〈《前書董仲舒曰：「王者正法度之宜，別上下之序，以防欲也。」〉

translations.literal: 〈The earlier Han book records Dong Zhongshu's words: 'The king rectifies the fitness of laws and institutions, distinguishes high from low, to guard against excess desire.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Dong Zhongshu's memorial urged kings to align statutes with moral hierarchy and curb appetite through ritual gradations.〉

sentences.zh: 嚴

translations.literal: Strictly
translations.idiomatic: Enforce

translations.literal: (read du)
translations.idiomatic: (phonetic gloss: read as du, earnest)

sentences.zh: [督]有司，必行其命。

translations.literal: [supervise] the responsible officials—execute the order without fail.
translations.idiomatic: empower overseers to execute the edict without compromise.

sentences.zh: 此則禁亂善俗足用之要。

translations.literal: This then is the essential for prohibiting disorder, improving custom, and sufficiency in use.
translations.idiomatic: That is the crux of stopping chaos, improving customs, and keeping the realm solvent.

sentences.zh: 奏聞，即弃官去。

translations.literal: When it was presented to the throne, he immediately abandoned office and left.
translations.idiomatic: Once the memorial reached the emperor, he resigned on the spot and departed.

sentences.zh: 後遭黨錮，隱於海上，又南遁漢濱，積十餘年，以著述為事，遂稱為碩儒。

translations.literal: Later, suffering the Partisan Prohibition, he hid on the sea, then fled south to the Han riverbanks; for more than ten years he took writing as his task, and came to be called a great Confucian.
translations.idiomatic: When the faction lists struck, he vanished to the coast, then drifted south along the Han for over a decade, devoting himself to scholarship until everyone called him a leading Ru.

sentences.zh: 黨禁解，五府並辟，司空袁逢舉有道，不應。

translations.literal: When the faction ban lifted, the Five Offices together summoned him; Minister of Works Yuan Feng recommended him as Morally Accomplished, but he did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: After the prohibition ended, all five executive bureaus courted him; Yuan Feng, Minister of Works, nominated him as a man of outstanding virtue, yet he refused.

sentences.zh: 及逢卒，爽制服三年，當世往往化以為俗。

translations.literal: When Feng died, Shuang wore mourning three years; the age often took this as custom.
translations.idiomatic: When Yuan Feng died, Shuang observed three years of mourning—soon contemporaries imitated him as fashion.

sentences.zh: 時人多不行妻服，雖在親憂猶有弔問喪疾者，又私謚其君父及諸名士，爽皆引據大義，正之經典，雖不悉變，亦頗有改。

translations.literal: People of the time often did not observe mourning for wives; even amid bereavement for parents there were still those who paid condolence visits or asked after illness; moreover they privately gave posthumous titles to their lords and fathers and various famed gentlemen—Shuang cited great principle and corrected them by the classics; although not everything changed, quite a bit was reformed.
translations.idiomatic: Most men still skipped wife-mourning and continued social calls even while mourning parents; private posthumous titles flourished for patrons and celebrities. Shuang cited canonical doctrine against each abuse—not every habit vanished, but many shifted.

sentences.zh: 〈喪服曰：「夫為妻齊縗杖開。

translations.literal: 〈The Sang fu says: 'The husband for the wife [wears] aligned sackcloth with staff gaps.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Mourning Dress canon prescribes even-hemp garments with split staff for a husband mourning his wife—

sentences.zh: 」禮記曰：「曾子問：『三年之喪吊乎？

translations.literal: ' The Liji says: 'Master Zeng asked: "During three years' mourning, does one pay condolence visits?"
translations.idiomatic: while the Record of Rites records Master Zeng asking whether condolence calls belong within three years' mourning—

sentences.zh: 』孔子曰：『禮以飾情。

translations.literal: ' Confucius said: 'Ritual ornaments emotion.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius answered that ritual shapes feeling—

sentences.zh: 三年之喪而吊哭，不亦虛乎！』」〉

translations.literal: ' To condole and weep during three years' mourning—is that not hollow!'")〉
translations.idiomatic: so to rush off condolence visits mid-mourning hollows the heart.'"〉

sentences.zh: 後公車徵為大將軍何進從事中郎。

translations.literal: Later he was summoned by the imperial coach and appointed aide-de-camp to Grand General He Jin.
translations.idiomatic: Later an imperial carriage summons made him staff adviser to Grand General He Jin.

sentences.zh: 進恐其不至，迎薦為侍中，及進敗而詔命中絕。

translations.literal: Jin feared he would not come and welcomed him with recommendation as palace attendant; when Jin fell, the edict appointments ceased midway.
translations.idiomatic: He Jin promoted him to palace attendant to secure his loyalty; after Jin's coup collapsed, the appointments stalled.

sentences.zh: 獻帝即立，董卓輔政，復徵之。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xian ascended the throne, Dong Zhuo directed policy and summoned him again.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xian's enthronement brought Dong Zhuo to power and another summons for Shuang.

sentences.zh: 爽欲遁命，吏持之急，不得去，因復就拜平原相。

translations.literal: Shuang wished to flee the commission; clerks pressed him urgently so he could not leave, and therefore again accepted appointment as chancellor of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Shuang meant to decline, but clerks hounded him until he could not escape and accepted the chancellorship of Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 行至宛陵，復追為光祿勳。

translations.literal: When his journey brought him to Wanling, he was again promoted to Superintendent of the Imperial Household.
translations.idiomatic: He had not yet reached his post when, at Wanling, an edict promoted him to Superintendent of the Imperial Household.

sentences.zh: 視事三日，進拜司空。

translations.literal: Three days after entering duty, he advanced to Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: Three days into office he was elevated to Minister of Works.

sentences.zh: 爽自被徵命及登臺司，九十五日。

translations.literal: From first summons to standing atop the Three Excellencies took ninety-five days.
translations.idiomatic: Ninety-five days elapsed between the first summons and his seat among the Three Excellencies.

sentences.zh: 因從遷都長安。

translations.literal: Thereupon he followed the removal of the capital to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: He then accompanied the court's forced move to Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 爽見董卓忍暴滋甚，必危社稷，其所辟舉皆取才略之士，將共圖之，亦與司徒王允及卓長史何顒等為內謀。

translations.literal: Shuang saw that Dong Zhuo's cruelty and violence grew daily and would endanger the altars of soil and grain; those he recruited were all men of talent and strategy, intending to plot together against Zhuo; he also secretly intrigued inside with Minister of Education Wang Yun and Dong Zhuo's chief clerk He Yong.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing Dong Zhuo's brutality swell until it threatened the dynasty, Shuang stacked his staff with capable tacticians to strike at Zhuo, coordinating secretly with Wang Yun and He Yong.

sentences.zh: 會病薨，年六十三。

translations.literal: Before long he died of illness, aged sixty-three.
translations.idiomatic: He died of illness soon afterward at sixty-three.

sentences.zh: 著禮、易傳、詩傳、尚書正經、春秋條例，又集漢事成敗可為鑒戒者，謂之《漢語》。

translations.literal: He authored works on the Rituals, commentaries on the Changes and the Songs, a standard recension of the Documents, and Chunqiu case precedents, and he also collected Han-dynasty episodes of success and failure that could serve as warning, entitling that compilation Han Discourses.
translations.idiomatic: His scholarly legacy spans ritual scholarship, Zhouyi and Songs commentaries, a critical edition of the Documents, Spring and Autumn precedents, plus an anthology of cautionary Han episodes published as Han Discourses.

sentences.zh: 又作公羊問及辯讖，並它所論敍，題為新書。

translations.literal: He also wrote Gongyang inquiries and disputations on prophecy texts, together with other essays, titled as New Writings.
translations.idiomatic: He likewise composed Gongyang dialogues, critiques of chenwei lore, and miscellaneous treatises published under the title New Writings.

sentences.zh: 凡百餘篇，今多所亡缺。

translations.literal: In all more than a hundred chapters; many are now lost or defective.
translations.idiomatic: Over a hundred scrolls once circulated; most are now fragmentary or gone.

sentences.zh: 兄子悅、彧並知名。

translations.literal: His nephews Yue and Yu were both celebrated.
translations.idiomatic: His nephews Xun Yue and Xun Yu both became famous.

sentences.zh: 彧自有傳。

translations.literal: Yu has his own biography.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Yu receives a separate biography.

sentences.zh: 論曰：荀爽、鄭玄、申屠蟠俱以儒行為處士，累征並謝病不詣。

translations.literal: The judgment says: Xun Shuang, Zheng Xuan, and Shentu Pan all lived as scholars-at-large on Confucian conduct; repeatedly summoned, they all pleaded illness and did not go.
translations.idiomatic: The historian reflects that Xun Shuang, Zheng Xuan, and Shentu Pan exemplified Confucian integrity in reclusion, refusing summons after summons with claims of illness.

sentences.zh: 及董卓當朝，復備禮召之。

translations.literal: When Dong Zhuo held the court, he again prepared full ritual and summoned them.
translations.idiomatic: When Dong Zhuo seized the government, he rolled out full ceremony to drag them in.

sentences.zh: 蟠、玄竟不屈以全其高。

translations.literal: Pan and Xuan to the end did not yield, preserving their loftiness.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Pan and Zheng Xuan never bent, guarding their moral height.

sentences.zh: 爽已黃發矣，獨至焉，未十旬而取卿相。

translations.literal: Shuang was already hoary-headed; he alone came—before ten tens of days he had taken minister and chancellor posts.
translations.idiomatic: Shuang alone answered the call, gray-haired yet advancing from summons to chief minister within ninety days.

sentences.zh: 意者疑其乖趣捨，余竊商其情，以為出處君子之大致也，平運則弘道以求志，陵夷則濡跡以匡時。

translations.literal: Some suspect he betrayed the calculus of withdrawal and office; I privately weigh his feeling and think going out or staying in captures the great tendency of the gentleman: in calm times he expands the Way to seek his aim; in decline he smears his traces to rescue the age.
translations.idiomatic: Critics call his choice inconsistent; I read his situation differently—the gentleman adapts: in peace he teaches to fulfill ambition; in collapse he may muddy his footprints to salvage the times.

sentences.zh: 〈濡跡，解見崔駰傳。〉

translations.literal: 〈Smearing traces—explanation seen in the biography of Cui Yin.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈"Smearing traces" is glossed in Cui Yin's biography.〉

sentences.zh: 荀公之急急自勵，其濡跡乎？

translations.literal: Did Duke Xun urgently brace himself—was that smearing his traces?
translations.idiomatic: Was Master Xun's anxious self-discipline that very tactic of deliberate compromise?

sentences.zh: 不然，何為違貞吉而履虎尾焉？

translations.literal: If not, why depart from steadfast good fortune and tread on the tiger's tail?
translations.idiomatic: If not, why abandon secure righteousness and walk on a tiger's tail?

sentences.zh: 〈易履卦曰：「履道坦坦，幽人貞吉。

translations.literal: 〈The Yi hexagram Lu says: 'Walking the path level and smooth—the recluse is steadfastly auspicious.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The hexagram Lu promises smooth roads and auspicious reclusion—

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「履虎尾，不咥人亨。

translations.literal: ' It also says: 'Treading on the tiger's tail—the tiger does not bite; fortune.'
translations.idiomatic: It also warns of stepping a tiger's tail without being devoured—success through peril—

sentences.zh: 」王輔嗣注云：「履虎尾者，言其危也。」〉

translations.literal: ' Wang Bi's commentary: 'Treading the tiger's tail speaks of danger.'〉
translations.idiomatic: Wang Bi notes that the image stresses mortal danger.〉

sentences.zh: 觀其遜言遷都之議，以救楊、黃之禍。

translations.literal: Observe his yielding words on moving the capital—thereby to save Yang Biao and Huang Wan from calamity.
translations.idiomatic: Consider how deferential rhetoric on relocating the capital shielded Yang Biao and Huang Wan.

sentences.zh: 〈楊彪、黃琬也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Yang Biao and Huang Wan.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The ministers Yang Biao and Huang Wan.〉

sentences.zh: 及後潛圖董氏，幾振國命，所謂「大直若屈」，道固逶迤也。

translations.literal: And afterward he secretly plotted against the Dong clan, nearly reviving the state's mandate—what is called 'the utterly straight seems bent'; the Way is indeed winding.
translations.idiomatic: Later he conspired against Dong Zhuo's faction and nearly restored the throne—the Daodejing's "great straightness seems twisted" fits; the Way moves in curves.

sentences.zh: 〈《老子》云：「大直若屈，大巧若拙。

translations.literal: 〈The Laozi says: 'Great straightness seems bent; great skill seems clumsy.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Laozi teaches that perfection looks awkward—

sentences.zh: 」逶迤，曲也。〉

translations.literal: ' Weiyi means curved.'〉
translations.idiomatic: and weiyi means twisting, winding motion.〉

sentences.zh: 悅字仲豫，儉之子也。

translations.literal: Yue, courtesy name Zhongyu, was Jian's son.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Yue, styled Zhongyu, was Xun Jian's son.

sentences.zh: 儉早卒。

translations.literal: Jian died early.
translations.idiomatic: His father died young.

sentences.zh: 悅年十二，能說春秋。

translations.literal: At twelve Yue could expound the Chunqiu.
translations.idiomatic: At twelve he could lecture on the Spring and Autumn Annals.

sentences.zh: 家貧無書，每之人閒，所見篇牘，一覽多能誦記。

translations.literal: The family was poor and lacked books; whenever he went among people, whatever documents he saw, one glance and he could mostly recite them from memory.
translations.idiomatic: Books were beyond the family's means, yet whatever text he glimpsed in town he memorized after a single reading.

sentences.zh: 性沉靜，美姿容，尤好著述。

translations.literal: His nature was calm and reserved, his bearing handsome; he especially loved writing.
translations.idiomatic: He was sober, handsome, and happiest composing.

sentences.zh: 靈帝時閹官用權，士多退身窮處，悅乃托疾隱居，時人莫之識，雖從弟彧特稱敬焉。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Ling the eunuchs wielded power; many gentlemen withdrew to poor obscurity—Yue therefore feigned illness and lived hidden; people of the time did not recognize him, though his cousin Yu especially honored him.
translations.idiomatic: As eunuchs dominated Lingdi's court, scholars fled to backwaters; Yue hid behind illness, unknown to most though his cousin Yu revered him.

sentences.zh: 初辟鎮東將軍曹操府，遷黃門侍郎。

translations.literal: First he was recruited to General Who Guards the East Cao Cao's headquarters, then promoted to palace attendant under the yellow gates.
translations.idiomatic: He began in Cao Cao's eastern-guard headquarters and rose to palace attendant.

sentences.zh: 獻帝頗好文學，悅與彧及少府孔融侍講禁中，旦夕談論。

translations.literal: Emperor Xian showed fondness for letters; Yue with Yu and Privy Treasurer Kong Rong lectured inside the forbidden precincts, debating morning and evening.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xian loved literature; Yue joined Yu and Privy Treasurer Kong Rong lecturing in the inner palace from dawn to dusk.

sentences.zh: 累遷秘書監、侍中。

translations.literal: He was repeatedly promoted to Director of the Palace Library and palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Promotions carried him to Director of the Palace Library and attendant-in-ordinary.

sentences.zh: 時政移曹氏，天子恭己而已。

translations.literal: At that time policy shifted to the Cao clan; the Son of Heaven merely cultivated himself.
translations.idiomatic: Real power had passed to the Caos while the emperor ritually "tended his person" alone.

sentences.zh: 悅志在獻替，而謀無所用，乃作申鑒五篇。

translations.literal: Yue's intent lay in offering criticism and substitution [of policy], but his counsel found no use; therefore he composed the Shenjian in five chapters.
translations.idiomatic: Yue meant to remonstrate and reform policy; thwarted, he wrote the five scrolls of Extended Reflections.

sentences.zh: 其所論辯，通見政體，既成而奏之。

translations.literal: What he debated runs through the body of governance; when finished he presented it.
translations.idiomatic: Its arguments map the full machinery of rule; when complete he laid it before the throne.

sentences.zh: 其大略曰：

translations.literal: Its outline says:
translations.idiomatic: The synopsis opens:

sentences.zh: 夫道之本，仁義而已矣。

translations.literal: As for the root of the Way, it is simply benevolence and moral rightness.
translations.idiomatic: The Way rests, at bottom, on humanity and justice.

sentences.zh: 〈《易》曰：「立人之道曰仁與義。」〉

translations.literal: 〈The Changes says: 'Establishing the Way for human beings is called benevolence and moral rightness.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Zhouyi defines the human Way as benevolence and righteousness.〉

sentences.zh: 五典以經之，群籍以緯之，詠之歌之，弦之舞之，前監既明，後復申之。

translations.literal: The Five Canons take it as warp, the multitude of writings as weft; they chant it in songs, play it on strings and dance it; former oversight being clear, later ages repeat it.
translations.idiomatic: The Five Classics weave it as warp, other texts as weft—sung, played, danced—and each generation reiterates the lesson.

sentences.zh: 故古之聖王，其於仁義也，申重而已。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage kings of antiquity, regarding benevolence and moral rightness, reiterated and stressed them nothing more.
translations.idiomatic: Ancient sage-kings did nothing but hammer humanity and justice again and again.

sentences.zh: 致政之術，先屏四患，乃崇五政。

translations.literal: The art of bringing governance lies first in screening out four calamities, then in elevating five policies.
translations.idiomatic: Good government first eliminates four scourges, then advances five positive measures.

sentences.zh: 一曰偽，二曰私，三曰放，四曰奢。

translations.literal: First, hypocrisy; second, selfishness; third, license; fourth, extravagance.
translations.idiomatic: They are hypocrisy, private interest, unrestraint, and luxury.

sentences.zh: 偽亂俗，私壞法，放越軌，奢敗制。

translations.literal: Hypocrisy disorders custom; selfishness ruins law; license oversteps norms; extravagance destroys regulation.
translations.idiomatic: Hypocrisy corrupts manners, selfishness breaks statutes, license leaps boundaries, luxury shatters sumptuary rules.

sentences.zh: 四者不除，則政末由行矣。

translations.literal: If the four are not removed, policy has no way to proceed.
translations.idiomatic: Until those four are cleared away, no reform can move.

sentences.zh: 夫俗亂則道荒，雖天地不得保其性矣；

translations.literal: When custom is disordered, the Way grows waste—even heaven and earth cannot preserve their natures;
translations.idiomatic: When customs rot, the moral Way starves—even heaven and earth lose their proper temper;

sentences.zh: 法壞則世傾，雖人主不得守其度矣；

translations.literal: when law is ruined, the age tilts—even the ruler cannot guard his measures;
translations.idiomatic: when law collapses, the age reels—even the throne cannot hold its standards;

sentences.zh: 軌越則禮亡，雖聖人不得全其道矣；

translations.literal: when norms are overstepped, ritual perishes—even sages cannot keep their Way whole;
translations.idiomatic: when boundaries blur, ritual dies—even sages cannot preserve the full Way;

sentences.zh: 制敗則欲肆，雖四表不得充其求矣。

translations.literal: when regulation fails, desire runs riot—even the four quarters cannot satisfy its demands.
translations.idiomatic: when institutions fail, appetite runs wild—not even the empire's wealth can feed it.

sentences.zh: 〈肆，放也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Si means 'release.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Si glosses as licentious excess.〉

sentences.zh: 是謂四患。

translations.literal: These are called the four calamities.
translations.idiomatic: Those four are the scourges.

sentences.zh: 興農桑以養其

translations.literal: Raise agriculture and sericulture to nourish their
translations.idiomatic: Promote farming and silk production to sustain the people's

translations.literal: (Interlinear note: read sheng as xing 'nature.')
translations.idiomatic: (Copyists gloss the broken phrase by reading sheng as xing, tying the line to human nature.)

sentences.zh: [生]，審好惡以正其俗，宣文教以章其化，立武備以秉以其威，明賞罰以統其法。

translations.literal: [life], examine likes and dislikes to rectify custom; proclaim literary teaching to manifest transformation; establish martial preparedness to wield its awe; clarify rewards and punishments to unify the laws.
translations.idiomatic: They nourish the people's livelihoods, discriminate good from evil to straighten custom, broadcast civilization to display moral transformation, keep arms in readiness to project awe, and make rewards and penalties transparent so the laws stand as one body.

sentences.zh: 是謂五政。

translations.literal: These are called the five policies.
translations.idiomatic: Those five measures complete the positive agenda.

sentences.zh: 人不畏死，不可懼以罪。

translations.literal: When people do not fear death, one cannot frighten them with crimes.
translations.idiomatic: Subjects numb to death ignore penal threats.

sentences.zh: 人不樂生，不可勸以善。

translations.literal: When people do not delight in life, one cannot exhort them with goodness.
translations.idiomatic: Those who see no joy in living cannot be moved toward virtue.

sentences.zh: 雖使契布五教，戲陶作士，政不行焉。

translations.literal: Even if you had Xie spread the five teachings and Gao Yao act as Minister of Justice, policy would not take effect.
translations.idiomatic: Let Shun's ministers preach the five relations and Gao Yao guard the five punishments—without hope, edicts still fail.

sentences.zh: 〈尚書舜謂契曰：「汝作司徒，敬敷五教在寬。

translations.literal: 〈The Shang shu: Shun said to Xie, 'You shall be Minister of Education—reverently diffuse the five teachings with generosity.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Documents records Shun telling Xie to spread the five moral teachings with lenience—

sentences.zh: 」謂戲陶曰：「汝作士，明於五刑。」〉

translations.literal: ' To Gao Yao he said, 'You shall be judge—be clear about the five punishments.'〉
translations.idiomatic: and charging Gao Yao to master the five penal statutes.'〉

sentences.zh: 故在上者先豐人財以定其志，帝耕籍田，後桑蠶宮，

translations.literal: Therefore those above first enrich people's wealth to settle their wills—the emperor plows the ceremonial field, the empress tends silkworms in the palace,
