translations.idiomatic: Rulers must therefore secure livelihoods before preaching virtue: the sovereign farms the sacred acre while the empress tends mulberries—

sentences.zh: 〈籍田事，解見明紀。

translations.literal: 〈Matters of the ceremonial field—explanation seen in the annals of Emperor Ming.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The plowed field is discussed in Emperor Ming's annals—

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「季春之月，后妃齋戒，親東向桑，以勸蠶事。

translations.literal: The Liji says: 'In the last month of spring, the consorts fast, face east, tend the mulberry, and encourage sericulture.
translations.idiomatic: the Book of Rites says the queen and consorts fast, face east, and pick mulberry to model silkworm work—

sentences.zh: 」古者天子諸侯必有公桑蠶室，近川而為之，宮仞有三尺也。〉

translations.literal: ' In antiquity Son of Heaven and feudal lords always had public mulberry and silkworm chambers, built near a stream, with earthen walls three ren high.'〉
translations.idiomatic: and that every court maintained public silkworm houses by running water, their walls three ren high.'〉

sentences.zh: 國無遊人，野無荒業，財不賈用，

translations.literal: In the state there are no idlers; in the fields no land lies waste; goods are not wantonly expended,
translations.idiomatic: No vagrants crowd the capital, no field lies fallow, and wealth is not thrown away frivolously—

sentences.zh: 〈言自足也。〉

translations.literal: 〈It means self-sufficiency.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈That is, the realm becomes self-sufficient.〉

sentences.zh: 力不妄加，以周人事。

translations.literal: labor is not applied at random, in order to complete human affairs.
translations.idiomatic: labor is never wasted, so the people's work actually meets their needs.

sentences.zh: 是謂養生。

translations.literal: This is called nurturing life.
translations.idiomatic: This is the policy of nurturing life.

sentences.zh: 〈周，給也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Zhou means 'supply.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Zhou here means to supply or provide for.〉

sentences.zh: 君子之所以動天地，應神明，正萬物而成王化者，必乎真定而已。

translations.literal: The reason the gentleman can move heaven and earth, respond to spirit luminaries, rectify the myriad things and complete kingly transformation lies surely in true determination alone.
translations.idiomatic: What lets the noble man stir Heaven and Earth, answer the spirits, set the myriad creatures right, and perfect royal transformation is steadfast authenticity.

sentences.zh: 故在上者審定好丑焉。

translations.literal: Therefore those above examine and settle what is beautiful or ugly.
translations.idiomatic: Hence superiors must judge clearly what counts as noble or base.

sentences.zh: 善惡要乎功罪，毀譽効於准驗。

translations.literal: Good and evil hinge on merit and crime; slander and praise find verification in evidence.
translations.idiomatic: Praise and blame track actual deeds; reputation must match proof.

sentences.zh: 聽言責事，舉名察實，無惑詐偽，以蕩眾心。

translations.literal: Listen to words but inspect deeds; lift names but examine realities—do not be deluded by deceit, lest you unsettle popular hearts.
translations.idiomatic: Test rhetoric against performance and titles against facts so fraud cannot shake public trust.

sentences.zh: 故事無不核，物無不切，善無不顯，惡無不章，俗無奸怪，民無淫風。

translations.literal: Therefore affairs are never unverified, things never unexamined, good never hidden, evil never unexposed, custom never freakish, the people never debauched.
translations.idiomatic: Then every deed is checked, every claim tested, virtue shines, vice is exposed, customs stay sane, and lewd fashions die.

sentences.zh: 百姓上下鶯利害之存乎己也，故肅恭其心，慎修其行，內不回惑，外無異望，則民志平矣。

translations.literal: The hundred clans, high and low, see that advantage and harm rest with themselves; therefore they solemnly compose their hearts, cautiously cultivate conduct, inwardly without doubt, outwardly without vain hopes—then popular aims are level.
translations.idiomatic: From court to village people realize gain and loss are their own doing; when they steady intent, polish conduct, quiet inner doubt, and curb vain ambition, public mood levels out.

sentences.zh: 是謂正俗。

translations.literal: This is called rectifying custom.
translations.idiomatic: That is the policy of rectifying customs.

sentences.zh: 君子以情用，小人以刑用。

translations.literal: The gentleman is employed through affection; the petty man through punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Great men respond to moral suasion; small men only to sanctions.

sentences.zh: 榮辱者，賞罰之精華也。

translations.literal: Glory and shame are the essence of rewards and punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Honor and disgrace distill reward and punishment into moral feeling.

sentences.zh: 故禮教榮辱，以加君子，化其情也；

translations.literal: Therefore ritual instruction in glory and shame is applied to the gentleman—to transform his disposition;
translations.idiomatic: Teach the gentleman with ritualized honor and shame so his emotions reform—

sentences.zh: 桎梏鞭撲，以加小人，化其刑也。

translations.literal: cangues and whip strokes are applied to the petty man—to transform him through punishment.
translations.idiomatic: but curb the petty man with fetters and flogging so fear reshapes him.

sentences.zh: 君子不犯辱，況於刑乎！

translations.literal: The gentleman does not incur shame—how much less punishment!
translations.idiomatic: A gentleman avoids dishonor altogether, let alone the stocks.

sentences.zh: 小人不忌刑，況於辱乎！

translations.literal: The petty man does not shrink from punishment—how much less shame!
translations.idiomatic: Petty men fear neither fetters nor stigma.

sentences.zh: 若教化之廢，推中人而墜於小人之域；

translations.literal: When moral instruction collapses, middling men tumble into the petty man's realm;
translations.idiomatic: When instruction fails, average men sink to the gutter—

sentences.zh: 教化之行，引中人而納於君子之塗。

translations.literal: when moral instruction flows, middling men are drawn onto the gentleman's path.
translations.idiomatic: when it thrives, average men climb toward nobility.

sentences.zh: 是謂章化。

translations.literal: This is called manifesting transformation.
translations.idiomatic: That is how transformation becomes visible.

sentences.zh: 〈章，明也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Zhang means 'clear.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Zhang glosses as making plain or luminous.〉

sentences.zh: 小人之情，緩則驕，驕則恣，恣則怨，怨則叛，危則謀亂，安則思欲，非威強無以懲之。

translations.literal: The petty man's disposition: when indulged he grows arrogant; arrogant, reckless; reckless, resentful; resentful, rebellious; in peril he plots rebellion; in peace he indulges desire—without awe and strength nothing can punish him.
translations.idiomatic: Small natures, once pampered, swell into arrogance, spite, mutiny, or reckless craving—only naked force checks them.

sentences.zh: 故在上者，必有武備，以戒不虞，以遏寇虐。

translations.literal: Therefore those above must have martial preparedness, to guard against the unforeseen and to stop invasion and cruelty.
translations.idiomatic: Those aloft need armed readiness to foil surprise attacks and crush marauders.

sentences.zh: 安居則寄之內政，有事則用之軍旅。

translations.literal: In peace lodge it in domestic administration; in crisis deploy it in the army.
translations.idiomatic: Embed drill in civil routine during peace; unleash it on campaign when war comes.

sentences.zh: 〈《國語》齊桓公問管仲曰：「國安可乎？

translations.literal: 〈The Guoyu: Duke Huan of Qi asked Guan Zhong, 'Can the state be secure?
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Discourses of States records Duke Huan asking Guan Zhong whether Lu could rest secure—

sentences.zh: 」管仲曰：「未可。

translations.literal: ' Guan Zhong said, 'Not yet.
translations.idiomatic: Guan answered, "Not yet."

sentences.zh: 君若正卒伍，修甲兵，則大國亦將修之，小國設備，可作內政而寄軍令焉。

translations.literal: If you align the companies and repair armor, great states will do the same; small states will arm—instead enact domestic policy while lodging military orders within it.
translations.idiomatic: "If you openly drill troops, rivals arm in kind; better embed army regulations inside civil reform so neighbors stay blind."

sentences.zh: 」注云：「

translations.literal: ' The commentary says: '
translations.idiomatic: The commentary continues: "

translations.literal: (zheng)
translations.idiomatic: (variant reading zheng)

sentences.zh: [政]，國政也。

translations.literal: [government] is statecraft.
translations.idiomatic: meaning domestic administration—

sentences.zh: 言修國政而寄軍令，鄰國不知。」〉

translations.literal: It speaks of cultivating statecraft while lodging military orders—neighboring states do not know.'〉
translations.idiomatic: training the state without advertising mobilization to outsiders.'〉

sentences.zh: 是謂秉威。

translations.literal: This is called wielding awe.
translations.idiomatic: That policy concentrates deterrent awe.

sentences.zh: 賞罰，政之柄也。

translations.literal: Rewards and punishments are the handles of governance.
translations.idiomatic: Reward and punishment are the twin levers of rule.

sentences.zh: 〈《韓子》曰：「二柄者，刑、德也。

translations.literal: 〈The Han Feizi says: 'The two handles are punishment and virtue.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Han Feizi calls them the two handles—punishment and kindness—

sentences.zh: 殺戮之謂刑，慶賞之謂德。」〉

translations.literal: Slaughter is called punishment; congratulatory reward is called virtue.'〉
translations.idiomatic: death dealing versus bounty bestowing.'〉

sentences.zh: 明賞必罰，審信慎令，賞以勸善，罰以懲惡。

translations.literal: Make rewards clear and punishments certain; examine trustworthiness and treat edicts cautiously—reward to encourage good, punish to chastise evil.
translations.idiomatic: Spell out incentives and sanctions, keep promises literal, and pair bounty with terror so virtue advances and vice recoils.

sentences.zh: 人主不妄賞，非徒愛其財也，賞妄行則善不勸矣。

translations.literal: The ruler does not reward at random—not merely from love of treasure; if rewards are reckless, good is not encouraged.
translations.idiomatic: Sovereigns withhold idle gifts not from stinginess—scatter rewards and merit loses meaning.

sentences.zh: 不妄罰，非矜其人也，罰妄行則惡不懲矣。

translations.literal: He does not punish at random—not from pity on others; if punishments are reckless, evil is not chastened.
translations.idiomatic: Nor does he strike capriciously from mercy—random blows let villainy run free.

sentences.zh: 賞不勸謂之止善，罰不懲謂之縱惡。

translations.literal: When rewards fail to encourage, that is called stopping good; when punishments fail to chastise, that is called indulging evil.
translations.idiomatic: Rewards that fire no zeal stall virtue; punishments that bite no deterrence license crime.

sentences.zh: 在上者能不止下為善，不縱下為惡，則國法立矣。

translations.literal: If those above can keep inferiors from abandoning good and indulging evil, then the state's laws stand.
translations.idiomatic: When the throne neither smothers virtue nor pampers vice, statute takes root.

sentences.zh: 是謂統法。

translations.literal: This is called unifying the laws.
translations.idiomatic: That is the policy of harmonizing law.

sentences.zh: 四患既蠲，五政又立，行之以誠，守之以固，簡而不怠，疏而不失，無為為之，使自施之，無事事之，使自交之。

translations.literal: Once the four calamities are removed and the five policies established, enact them with sincerity, guard them with constancy, concise yet untiring, loose yet without loss—act through non-action so things accomplish themselves, administer through non-administration so they naturally relate.
translations.idiomatic: With scourges gone and tools in place, practice them faithfully: stay lean yet tireless, delegate yet monitor—govern by non-interference so tasks finish themselves and relationships self-order.

sentences.zh: 〈《老子》曰：「為無為，事無事。

translations.literal: 〈The Laozi says: 'Act through non-action; administer through non-administration.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Laozi teaches acting without forcing—

sentences.zh: 」又曰「故德交歸」也。〉

translations.literal: ' It also says 'therefore virtue returns together.'〉
translations.idiomatic: so cumulative power flows back of its own accord.'〉

sentences.zh: 不肅而成，不嚴而化，垂拱揖讓，而海內平矣。

translations.literal: Without severity completion comes; without harshness transformation arrives—draping sleeves in reverent ease, the realm within the seas is pacified.
translations.idiomatic: Order emerges without crackdowns, customs soften without terror—the ruler folds hands on the dais and the realm rests.

sentences.zh: 是謂為政之方。

translations.literal: This is called the method of governing.
translations.idiomatic: That is the art of true governance.

sentences.zh: 又言：

translations.literal: He further stated:
translations.idiomatic: The memorial continues:

sentences.zh: 尚主之制非古。

translations.literal: The institution of honoring the imperial daughter as wife is not ancient.
translations.idiomatic: Elevating princess-brides above their husbands has no classical warrant.

sentences.zh: 厘降二女，陶唐之典。

translations.literal: Solemnly sending down the two daughters is the canon of lofty Tang.
translations.idiomatic: Yao's solemn transfer of his daughters embodies Tang-era orthodoxy.

sentences.zh: 歸妹元吉，帝乙之訓。

translations.literal: 'Returning the maiden—supreme good fortune' is the instruction of Di Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Di Yi's oracle about marrying out the princess teaches supreme fortune.

sentences.zh: 王姬歸齊，宗周之禮。

translations.literal: The king's daughter marrying into Qi follows Zhou ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Royal daughters entering Qi follow Western Zhou protocol.

sentences.zh: 以陰乘陽違天，以婦陵夫違人。

translations.literal: For yin to ride yang transgresses Heaven; for wives to lord over husbands transgresses humanity.
translations.idiomatic: Letting yin dominate yang offends Heaven; wives commanding husbands offends human ethics.

sentences.zh: 違天不祥，違人不義。

translations.literal: To transgress Heaven is inauspicious; to transgress humanity is unrighteous.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven punishes inversion of cosmic order; society condemns moral inversion.

sentences.zh: 又古者天子諸侯有事，必告於廟。

translations.literal: Moreover in antiquity Son of Heaven and feudal lords, whenever there was affair, always announced it in the ancestral temple.
translations.idiomatic: Ancient sovereigns and nobles reported every major act to the ancestral shrine.

sentences.zh: 朝有二史，左史記言，右史書事。

translations.literal: At court were two scribes: the left scribe recorded words, the right scribe recorded deeds.
translations.idiomatic: Two historians stood at court—left chronicling speeches, right chronicling acts.

sentences.zh: 〈《禮記》曰「天子朝日於東門之外，聽朔於南門之外，閏月則闔門左扉，立於其中，動則左史書之，言則右史書之」也。〉

translations.literal: 〈The Liji says: 'The Son of Heaven greets the sun outside the east gate, listens to the new moon outside the south gate; in intercalary months he closes the left leaf of the gate, stands within it—when he moves, the left scribe records it; when he speaks, the right scribe records it.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Book of Rites prescribes exactly how sunrise audiences and monthly audiences were logged by paired historians.〉

sentences.zh: 事為春秋，言為尚書。

translations.literal: Deeds become the Chunqiu; words become the Shang shu.
translations.idiomatic: Events fed the Spring and Autumn Annals; pronouncements became the Book of Documents.

sentences.zh: 君舉必記，善惡成敗，無不存焉。

translations.literal: The ruler's every act must be recorded; good and evil, success and failure, none are omitted.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing the monarch does escapes the log—triumph, disaster, virtue, vice.

sentences.zh: 下及士庶，苟有茂異，咸在載籍。

translations.literal: Descending to gentry and commoners, if there be outstanding talent, all appear in the records.
translations.idiomatic: Commoners of striking merit likewise enter the chronicle.

sentences.zh: 或欲顯而不得，或欲隱而名章。

translations.literal: Some wish to shine yet fail; some wish to hide yet their names blaze.
translations.idiomatic: Some seek fame and miss it; some court obscurity and become notorious.

sentences.zh: 得失一朝，而榮辱千載。

translations.literal: Gain or loss in one morning; glory or shame for a thousand years.
translations.idiomatic: A single morning's choice stamps millennial honor or infamy.

sentences.zh: 善人勸焉，淫人懼焉。

translations.literal: Good men are encouraged; dissolute men fear.
translations.idiomatic: The worthy strive harder; the wanton tremble.

sentences.zh: 〈淫，過也。

translations.literal: 〈Yin means excess.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Yin glosses as moral excess or dissipation—

sentences.zh: 《左氏傳》曰「或求名而不得，或欲蓋而名章，書齊豹盜三叛人名，以懲不義」也。〉

translations.literal: The Zuoshi zhuan says: 'Some seek a name and fail; some try to cover yet their names blaze—recording the brigand Qi Bao and three rebels punishes unrighteousness.'〉
translations.idiomatic: the Zuo Tradition notes men who court fame yet earn infamy, citing Qi Bao as warning against injustice.'〉

sentences.zh: 宜於今者備置史官，掌其典文，紀其行事。

translations.literal: What fits the present is fully to appoint historians, charge them with canonical texts, and record conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Today we should staff historians, give them access to archives, and chronicle every deed.

sentences.zh: 每於歲盡，舉之尚書。

translations.literal: Each year at year's end, submit them to the Imperial Secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: Each winter submit compiled drafts to the Secretariat.

sentences.zh: 以助賞罰，以弘法教。

translations.literal: Thereby to aid rewards and punishments and to amplify civil instruction.
translations.idiomatic: Those records would reinforce justice and spread moral education.

sentences.zh: 帝覽而善之。

translations.literal: The emperor read it and praised it.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Xian read the memorial and approved.

sentences.zh: 帝好典籍，常以班固《漢書》文繁難省，乃令悅依左氏傳體以為《漢紀》三十篇，詔尚書給筆札。

translations.literal: The emperor loved classical writings; he often found Ban Gu's Han shu verbose and hard to review, and therefore ordered Yue to compose Han Ji in thirty chapters on the Zuo zhuan model—the edict supplied the Secretariat brushes and paper.
translations.idiomatic: Because Emperor Xian prized literature yet found Ban Gu's History of the Han unwieldy, he commissioned Yue to distill thirty chapters of Han Annals in Zuo-style annals, issuing stationery from the Secretariat.

sentences.zh: 辭約事詳，論辯多美。

translations.literal: The wording is concise yet events are detailed; much of the argumentation is excellent.
translations.idiomatic: The prose stays lean while incidents stay vivid; many judgments sing.

sentences.zh: 其序之曰：「昔在上聖，惟建皇極，經緯天地，觀象立法，乃作書契，以通宇宙，揚於王庭，厥用大焉。

translations.literal: Its preface says: 'Formerly the supreme sages established the royal utmost, ordered heaven and earth, observed images and set laws, then created writing to communicate with the universe—proclaimed in the royal court, its use was great.
translations.idiomatic: Yue's preface opens: ancient sages framed the cosmic pivot, aligned heaven and earth, read omens, codified law, invented script to link all under heaven—

sentences.zh: 先王光演大業，肆於時夏。

translations.literal: The former kings brilliantly unfolded the great enterprise and displayed it in this summer realm.
translations.idiomatic: Former kings broadcast their grand mandate across the Chinese heartland—

sentences.zh: 〈詩周頌曰：「我求懿德，肆於時夏。

translations.literal: 〈The Shi Zhou song says: 'We sought virtuous power and displayed it in this summer realm.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Zhou hymn sings that King Wu sought noble virtue and proclaimed it to the summer lands—

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄注曰：「懿，美也。

translations.literal: ' Zheng Xuan annotates: 'Yi means beautiful.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan glosses yi as beautiful—

sentences.zh: 肆，陳也。

translations.literal: Si means array.
translations.idiomatic: si as to display—

sentences.zh: 我，武王也。

translations.literal: I refers to King Wu.
translations.idiomatic: and "I" names King Wu—

sentences.zh: 求美德之士而任用之，故陳於是夏而歌之也。」〉

translations.literal: He sought knights of beautiful virtue and employed them; therefore he displayed this in the summer and sang it.'〉
translations.idiomatic: having enlisted worthy men, he celebrated them in seasonal hymns.'〉

sentences.zh: 亦惟厥後，永世作典。

translations.literal: Likewise their successors forever made canons.
translations.idiomatic: Their heirs likewise forged enduring classical models.

sentences.zh: 夫立典有五志焉：一曰達道義，二曰章法式，三曰通古今，四曰著功勳，五曰表賢能。

translations.literal: Establishing a canon has five aims: first, communicating moral principle; second, clarifying models and laws; third, linking past and present; fourth, recording merit; fifth, showcasing worthies.
translations.idiomatic: A great chronicle serves five ends—articulating principle, codifying institutions, linking eras, memorializing achievement, and spotlighting talent.

sentences.zh: 於是天人之際，事物之宜，粲然顯著，罔不備矣。

translations.literal: Thereupon between Heaven and man, the fitness of affairs and things, shines clearly brilliant—nothing is not prepared.
translations.idiomatic: Then the fit between heaven and humanity, act and outcome, stands luminous—nothing omitted.

sentences.zh: 世濟其軌，不隕其業。

translations.literal: Age after age they fulfilled that track without dropping the enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: Each reign carried the project forward without letting it fall.

sentences.zh: 〈濟，成也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Ji means 'complete.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Ji means to accomplish or carry through.〉

sentences.zh: 損益盈虛，與時消息。

translations.literal: Diminish and increase, fullness and emptiness, wax and wane with the seasons.
translations.idiomatic: Plenty and want ebb and flow with the times.

sentences.zh: 臧否不同，其揆一也。

translations.literal: Praise and blame differ, yet the measure is one.
translations.idiomatic: Verdicts vary, but the yardstick stays constant.

sentences.zh: 漢四百有六載，撥亂反正，統武興文，永惟祖宗之洪業，思光啟乎萬嗣。

translations.literal: Han endured four hundred six years—restoring order from chaos, gathering martial glory into civil brilliance—forever pondering the vast enterprise of the ancestors, intending to enlighten myriad heirs.
translations.idiomatic: Across four hundred six Han years, rulers turned rebellion into restoration, traded swords for books, and pondered how to widen the ancestors' light for endless successors.

sentences.zh: 聖上穆然，惟文之恤，瞻前顧後，是紹是繼，闡崇大猷，命立國典。

translations.literal: The sage emperor is solemn, anxious solely for culture—looking backward and forward, continuing and inheriting, exalting the great plan—ordering the establishment of a national canon.
translations.idiomatic: Our sage sovereign broods on letters alone, scanning past and future to enlarge the great blueprint and commission a state history.

sentences.zh: 於是綴□舊書，以述漢紀。

translations.literal: Thereupon he arranged [missing graph] the old documents to narrate the Han ji.
translations.idiomatic: From scattered archives he fashioned the Han Annals.

sentences.zh: 中興以前，明主賢臣得失之軌，亦足以觀矣。」

translations.literal: Before the restoration, the tracks of gain and loss of enlightened sovereigns and worthy ministers are also sufficient for contemplation.'
translations.idiomatic: The deeds of wise rulers and loyal ministers from the era before Guangwu's revival already offer ample warning and example.'

sentences.zh: 又著崇德、正論及諸論數十篇。

translations.literal: He also authored Chongde, Zheng lun, and several tens of other treatises.
translations.idiomatic: He also wrote Exalting Virtue, Correct Disputation, and dozens of further essays.

sentences.zh: 年六十二，建安十四年卒。

translations.literal: At age sixty-two he died in the fourteenth year of Jian'an.
translations.idiomatic: He died at sixty-two in Jian'an 14.

sentences.zh: 韓韶字仲黃，穎川舞陽人也。

translations.literal: Han Shao, courtesy name Zhonghuang, was a man of Wuyang in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Han Shao, styled Zhonghuang, came from Wuyang in Yingchuan Commandery.

sentences.zh: 少仕郡，辟司徒府。

translations.literal: In youth he served the commandery and was recruited to the Minister of Education's office.
translations.idiomatic: He began in local office and won appointment to the Excellency of Works.

sentences.zh: 時太山賊公孫舉偽號歷年，守令不能破散，多為坐法。

translations.literal: At that time the Mount Tai bandit Gongsun Ju had falsely declared a reign title for many years; the defending magistrates could not break and scatter them, and many were punished according to law.
translations.idiomatic: While the Taishan rebel Gongsun Ju styled himself ruler year after year, county defenders failed to crush him and many magistrates paid with their careers.

sentences.zh: 尚書選三府掾能理劇者，乃以韶為贏長。

translations.literal: The Imperial Secretariat selected from Three Offices aides capable of handling acute crises, and therefore appointed Shao magistrate of Ying.
translations.idiomatic: The Secretariat picked a crisis-tested aide from the Three Offices and named Shao magistrate of Ying county.

sentences.zh: 〈贏，縣，故城在今兗州博城縣東北。〉

translations.literal: 〈Ying is a county; its old walled town lies northeast of Bocheng town in Yanzhou today.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Ying county stood northeast of present Bocheng in Yanzhou.〉

sentences.zh: 賊聞其賢，相戒不入贏境。

translations.literal: The bandits heard of his worth and mutually warned one another not to enter Ying's borders.
translations.idiomatic: Word of his integrity kept brigands from crossing into Ying.

sentences.zh: 余縣多被寇盜，廢耕桑，其流入縣界求索衣糧者甚眾。

translations.literal: Neighboring counties suffered many raids by bandits; farming and sericulture were abandoned; those who flowed into the county boundary seeking clothing and food were very numerous.
translations.idiomatic: Raiders had ruined neighboring farms; refugees poured across the border begging food and clothing.

sentences.zh: 韶愍其饑困，乃開倉賑之，所稟贍萬餘戶。

translations.literal: Shao pitied their hunger and distress and opened the granaries to relieve them; those supplied amounted to more than ten thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Shao opened the granaries without hesitation, feeding well over ten thousand households.

sentences.zh: 主者爭謂不可。

translations.literal: Those in charge disputed together that it could not be done.
translations.idiomatic: His staff insisted the law forbade it.

sentences.zh: 韶曰：「長活溝壑之人，而以此伏罪，含笑入地矣。

translations.literal: Shao said: 'If by lengthening the lives of men who would die in ditches I incur punishment for this, I will enter the earth with a smile.'
translations.idiomatic: Shao replied, "If feeding starving refugees costs me my head, I will meet the executioner grinning."

sentences.zh: 」太守素知韶名德，竟無所坐。

translations.literal: The grand administrator long knew Shao's reputation and virtue, and in the end imposed no blame.
translations.idiomatic: The prefect, who respected Shao already, took no action.

sentences.zh: 以病卒官。

translations.literal: He died of illness in office.
translations.idiomatic: He died in harness.

sentences.zh: 同郡李膺、陳寔、杜密、荀淑等為立碑頌焉。

translations.literal: Li Ying, Chen Shi, Du Mi, Xun Shu and others of the same commandery erected a stele praising him.
translations.idiomatic: Li Ying, Chen Shi, Du Mi, Xun Shu, and other local luminaries raised a monument to his memory.

sentences.zh: 子融，字符長。

translations.literal: His son was Rong, courtesy name Zhangchang.
translations.idiomatic: His son Han Rong bore the style Zhangchang.

sentences.zh: 少能辯理而不為章句學。

translations.literal: In youth he could debate principles but did not pursue textual gloss scholarship.
translations.idiomatic: He argued doctrine fluently yet spurned fussy philology.

sentences.zh: 聲名甚盛，五府並辟。

translations.literal: His fame was very great; the Five Offices together recruited him.
translations.idiomatic: His reputation drew simultaneous summons from all five executive bureaus.

sentences.zh: 獻帝初，至太僕。

translations.literal: Early in Emperor Xian's reign he reached Grand Coachman.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Xian's reign he rose to Grand Coachman.

sentences.zh: 年七十卒。

translations.literal: He died at seventy.
translations.idiomatic: He died at seventy.

sentences.zh: 鍾皓字季明，穎川長社人也。

translations.literal: Zhong Hao, courtesy name Jiming, was a man of Changshe in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Hao, styled Jiming, came from Changshe in Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 為郡著姓，世善刑律。

translations.literal: His clan was a prominent surname of the commandery; for generations they excelled at penal law.
translations.idiomatic: The Zhongs were a leading clan and for generations had mastered penal codes.

sentences.zh: 皓少以篤行稱，公府連辟，為二兄未仕，避隱密山，

translations.literal: Hao in youth was famed for earnest conduct; the ducal offices repeatedly summoned him; because his two elder brothers had not yet taken office, he avoided and hid on Mount Mi,
translations.idiomatic: Though offices courted him for sterling conduct, he fled to Mount Mi until his elder brothers could precede him into service—

sentences.zh: 〈密縣山也。〉

translations.literal: 〈It is a mountain in Mi county.〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈That range lies in Mi county.〉

sentences.zh: 以詩律教授門徒千餘人。

translations.literal: He taught over a thousand disciples poetry and law.
translations.idiomatic: there he taught more than a thousand students the Songs and statute law.

sentences.zh: 同郡陳寔，年不及皓，皓引與為友。

translations.literal: Chen Shi of the same commandery was younger in years than Hao, yet Hao drew him in as friend.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Shi, younger than Hao, became his intimate despite the age gap.

sentences.zh: 皓為郡功曹，會辟司徒府，臨辭，太守問：「誰可代卿者？

translations.literal: When Hao was merit-assessor of the commandery, he was summoned to the Minister of Education's office; on the verge of departure, the grand administrator asked: 'Who can replace you?'
translations.idiomatic: Summoned from county merit-assessor to the Works ministry, he was asked who should succeed him.

sentences.zh: 」皓曰：「明府欲必得其人，西門亭長陳寔可。

translations.literal: Hao said: 'If my lord must indeed obtain the right man, Chief of the West Gate Pavilion Chen Shi will do.'
translations.idiomatic: Hao named Chen Shi, chief of the west-gate post, as the only worthy successor.

sentences.zh: 」寔聞之，曰：「鍾君似不察人，不知何獨識我？

translations.literal: When Shi heard this he said: 'Lord Zhong seems not to scrutinize people—why does he alone recognize me?'
translations.idiomatic: Chen wondered aloud why Zhong Hao, supposedly poor at reading men, singled him out.

sentences.zh: 」皓頃之自劾去。

translations.literal: Before long Hao impeached himself and left.
translations.idiomatic: Hao soon resigned to make way.

sentences.zh: 前後九辟公府，征為廷尉正、博士、林慮長，皆不就。

translations.literal: Nine times in succession he was summoned by ducal offices; he was appointed rectifier for the commandant of justice, erudite, and magistrate of Linyu—he accepted none.
translations.idiomatic: Nine national summonses followed—for Supreme Judge clerk, academician, Linyu magistrate—and he refused each.

sentences.zh: 時皓及荀淑並為士大夫所歸慕。

translations.literal: At that time Hao and Xun Shu were both objects of gentry admiration.
translations.idiomatic: He and Xun Shu became the moral poles that literati orbited.

sentences.zh: 李膺常歎曰：「荀君清識難尚，鍾君至德可師。」

translations.literal: Li Ying often sighed: 'Lord Xun's clear judgment is hard to match; Lord Zhong's consummate virtue can be taken as teacher.'
translations.idiomatic: Li Ying sighed that Xun Shu's discernment was unrivaled and Zhong Hao's character worthy of emulation.

sentences.zh: 皓兄子瑾母，膺之姑也。

translations.literal: The mother of Hao's elder brother's son Jin was Ying's aunt.
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Jin's mother was Li Ying's aunt.

sentences.zh: 瑾好學慕古，有退讓風，與膺同年，俱有聲名。

translations.literal: Jin loved learning and admired antiquity; he had a retiring modest manner; he was the same age as Ying and both had reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Jin revered antiquity, lived modestly, shared Ying's age cohort, and matched his fame.

sentences.zh: 膺祖太尉修，常言：「瑾似我家性，邦有道不廢，邦無道免於刑戮。

translations.literal: Ying's grandfather the Grand Commandant Xiu often said: 'Jin resembles the temperament of our house—when the state has the Way he is not cast aside; when the state lacks the Way he escapes execution.
translations.idiomatic: Ying's grandfather Grand Commandant Li Xiu said Jin carried the family temperament—useful when the court pursued virtue, safe when it turned cruel—

sentences.zh: 」復以膺妹妻之。

translations.literal: ' He moreover gave Ying's younger sister to him in marriage.
translations.idiomatic: so Ying married his younger sister to Jin.

sentences.zh: 瑾辟州府，未嘗屈志。

translations.literal: Jin was summoned to provincial and ducal offices but never bent his will.
translations.idiomatic: Provincial and central offices courted Jin, yet he never compromised.

sentences.zh: 膺謂之曰：「孟子以為『人無是非之心，非人也』。

translations.literal: Ying said to him: 'Mencius thought that "a man without the heart of right and wrong is not a man."'
translations.idiomatic: Ying challenged him with Mencius: "Lacking the sense of shame and right is inhuman."

sentences.zh: 〈《孟子》曰：「人無惻隱之心，非人也。

translations.literal: 〈The Mencius says: 'If a man lacks the heart of compassion, he is not a man.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Mencius lists hearts of compassion—

sentences.zh: 無羞惡之心，非人也。

translations.literal: ' If he lacks the heart of shame and disgust, he is not a man.
translations.idiomatic: of shame—

sentences.zh: 無辭讓之心，非人也。

translations.literal: ' If he lacks the heart of deference, he is not a man.
translations.idiomatic: of courtesy—

sentences.zh: 無是非之心，非人也。」〉

translations.literal: ' If he lacks the heart of right and wrong, he is not a man.'〉
translations.idiomatic: and of moral discernment—without these one is not human.'〉

sentences.zh: 弟何期不與孟軻同邪？

translations.literal: Why does younger brother not stand with Mencius?
translations.idiomatic: Why, brother, do you fall short of Mencius's standard?

sentences.zh: 」瑾常以膺言白皓。

translations.literal: ' Jin often reported Ying's words to Hao.
translations.idiomatic: Jin relayed the rebuke to Zhong Hao.

sentences.zh: 皓曰：「昔國武子好昭人過，以致怨本。

translations.literal: Hao said: 'Formerly Guo Wuzi liked to expose others' faults, thereby inviting resentment at the root.
translations.idiomatic: Hao answered, "Guo Wuzi invited ruin by broadcasting others' sins—

sentences.zh: 〈國武子，齊大夫。

translations.literal: 〈Guo Wuzi was a grandee of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Guo Wuzi served Qi—

sentences.zh: 齊慶克通於齊君之母，國武子知之而責慶克，夫人遂譖武子而逐之。

translations.literal: Qing Ke committed adultery with the Qi ruler's mother; Guo Wuzi knew it and reproached Qing Ke; the lady then slandered Wuzi and had him banished.
translations.idiomatic: when he exposed Qing Ke's affair with the duchess, she drove him out—

sentences.zh: 事見《左傳》。〉

translations.literal: The matter is seen in the Zuoshi zhuan.'〉
translations.idiomatic: the Zuo Tradition tells the tale.'〉

sentences.zh: 卒保身全家，爾道為貴。

translations.literal: In the end he preserved body and whole family—your course esteems that.
translations.idiomatic: Survival of kin matters most in such ages.

sentences.zh: 」其體訓所安，多此類也。

translations.literal: ' The substance of his instruction and comfort was mostly of this kind.
translations.idiomatic: Most of Hao's counseling sounded this theme.

sentences.zh: 年六十九，終於家。

translations.literal: At sixty-nine he ended at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home at sixty-nine.

sentences.zh: 諸儒頌之曰：「林慮懿德，非禮不處。

translations.literal: The scholars praised him in verse: 'Linyu's splendid virtue—he will not dwell save by ritual.
translations.idiomatic: A collective elegy sang: "In Linyu he kept unsullied virtue, lodging only where ritual allowed—

sentences.zh: 悅此詩書，弦琴樂古。

translations.literal: He delighted in these poems and documents, strummed zither and rejoiced in antiquity.
translations.idiomatic: loving the classics and zither, rejoicing in ancient models—

sentences.zh: 五就州招，九應台輔。

translations.literal: Five times the province summoned him; nine times the terrace ministers called.
translations.idiomatic: five provincial summonses, nine invitations from the high ministries—

sentences.zh: 逡巡王命，卒歲容與。」

translations.literal: He lingered over royal commands and ended his years at ease.'
translations.idiomatic: yet he dallied with imperial mandates and finished life in quiet ease.'"

sentences.zh: 皓孫繇，建安中為司隸校尉。

translations.literal: Hao's grandson You, in the Jian'an period was Metropolitan Superintendent.
translations.idiomatic: His grandson Zhong You became metropolitan commandant under Jian'an.

sentences.zh: 〈海內先賢傳曰：「繇字符常，郡主簿迪之子也。

translations.literal: 〈The Haixian xianxian zhuan says: 'You, courtesy name Yuanchang, was son of merit clerk Di.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Regional lore records You as Di's son, styled Yuanchang—

sentences.zh: 」魏志曰：「舉孝廉為尚書郎，辟三府為廷尉正、黃門侍郎。」〉

translations.literal: ' The Wei zhi says: 'Recommended filial and incorrupt as Imperial Secretariat gentleman; summoned to three offices as rectifier for the commandant of justice and palace attendant.'〉
translations.idiomatic: while the Wei history lists his rise through filial recommendation and palace posts.'〉

sentences.zh: 陳寔字仲弓，穎川許人也。

translations.literal: Chen Shi, courtesy name Zhonggong, was a man of Xu in Yingchuan.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Shi, styled Zhonggong, came from Xu in Yingchuan.

sentences.zh: 出於單微。

translations.literal: He came from humble singleness.
translations.idiomatic: He began from the humblest line.

sentences.zh: 自為兒童，雖在戲弄，為等類所歸。

translations.literal: From childhood, even in play, his peers deferred to him.
translations.idiomatic: Even as a child his playmates treated him as leader.

sentences.zh: 少作縣吏，常給事廝役，後為都亭

translations.literal: In youth he was a county clerk, often detailed to menial service; later he became aide at the metropolitan pavilion
translations.idiomatic: He started as county clerk running errands, then clerk at the capital district pavilion

translations.literal: (variant reading ci)
translations.idiomatic: (copyists vary the graph for the pavilion title)

sentences.zh: 佐。

translations.literal: assistant.
translations.idiomatic: watch-post.

sentences.zh: 而有志好學，坐立誦讀。

translations.literal: Yet he had aspiration and loved learning, sitting or standing reciting.
translations.idiomatic: Ambition drove him to study seated or standing.

sentences.zh: 縣令鄧邵試與語，奇之，聽受業太學。

translations.literal: Magistrate Deng Shao tried conversation with him and marveled; allowed him to study at the Imperial University.
translations.idiomatic: Magistrate Deng Shao, struck by his wit, sponsored study at the Imperial Academy.

sentences.zh: 後令復召為吏，乃避隱陽城山中。

translations.literal: Later the magistrate again summoned him as clerk; he therefore avoided and hid in Yangcheng mountain.
translations.idiomatic: When the magistrate pressed him back into clerking, he fled to Yangcheng Mountain.

sentences.zh: 時有殺人者，同縣楊吏以疑寔，縣遂逮系，考掠無實，而後得出。

translations.literal: At that time there was one who killed a man; a clerk named Yang in the same county suspected Shi; the county therefore seized and bound him, tortured and flogged without substantiation, and afterward he got out.
translations.idiomatic: A murder implicated him falsely; clerks jailed and beat him until proof failed and they released him.

sentences.zh: 及為督郵，乃密托許令，禮召楊吏。

translations.literal: When he became regional inspector's clerk, he secretly entrusted the magistrate of Xu to ceremonially summon Clerk Yang.
translations.idiomatic: As inspector's aide he persuaded Xu's magistrate to honor Summons Yang formally.

sentences.zh: 遠近聞者，鹹歎服之。

translations.literal: Those near and far who heard of it all sighed in admiration.
translations.idiomatic: News of that generosity won admiration far and wide.

sentences.zh: 家貧，復為郡西門亭長，尋轉功曹。

translations.literal: The family was poor; again he became chief of the west gate pavilion for the commandery, soon transferred to merit-assessor.
translations.idiomatic: Poverty returned him to west-gate chief, then merit-assessor.

sentences.zh: 時中常侍侯覽托太守高倫用吏，倫教署為文學掾。

translations.literal: At that time Regular Palace Attendant Hou Lan entrusted the grand administrator Gao Lun to appoint an official; Lun issued instructions to appoint [someone] as literary aide.
translations.idiomatic: Eunuch Hou Lan leaned on Prefect Gao Lun to place a client as literary aide.

sentences.zh: 寔知非其人，懷檄請見。

translations.literal: Shi knew he was not the right man; carrying the document concealed he requested audience.
translations.idiomatic: Knowing the nominee unfit, Shi tucked the appointment slip under his robe and sought Gao Lun.

sentences.zh: 〈檄，板書。

translations.literal: 〈Xi is writing on a wooden tablet.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Xi denotes an inscribed wooden slip—

sentences.zh: 謂以高倫之教書之於檄而懷之者，懼洩事也。〉

translations.literal: It means writing Excellency Gao Lun's instruction on the xi and carrying it—fearing to leak the affair.'〉
translations.idiomatic: here Shi's hidden tablet preserved Lun's order while avoiding scandal.'〉

sentences.zh: 言曰：「此人不宜用，而侯常侍不可違。

translations.literal: He said: 'This man is not suitable to employ, yet Regular Attendant Hou cannot be disobeyed.
translations.idiomatic: Shi said, "The candidate is worthless, yet we cannot defy Hou Lan—

sentences.zh: 寔乞從外署，不足以塵明德。

translations.literal: I beg to assign him from outside posting—insufficient to tarnish your bright virtue.'
translations.idiomatic: assign me to fill the post abroad so your integrity stays blameless.'"

sentences.zh: 」倫從之。

translations.literal: ' Lun followed him.
translations.idiomatic: Lun agreed.

sentences.zh: 〈請從外署之舉，不欲陷倫於請托也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Requesting outside assignment—did not wish to trap Lun in patronage.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Shi absorbed Hou's patronage fault himself so Lun could remain clean.〉

sentences.zh: 於是鄉論怪其非舉，寔終無所言。

translations.literal: Thereupon local opinion wondered at his not recommending [properly]; Shi in the end said nothing.
translations.idiomatic: Gossip blamed Shi for a bad nomination; he stayed silent.

sentences.zh: 倫後被征為尚書，郡中士大夫送至輪氏傳捨。

translations.literal: Later Lun was summoned as Imperial Secretariat Director; commandery gentlemen escorted him to the relay inn at Lunshi.
translations.idiomatic: When Lun rose to Secretariat chief, local elites saw him off at Lunshi station.

sentences.zh: 〈輪氏，縣名，屬穎川郡，今故高陽縣是。〉

translations.literal: 〈Lunshi is a county name belonging to Yingchuan commandery; today's former Gaoyang county.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Lunshi lay in Yingchuan—modern Gaoyang township.〉

sentences.zh: 倫謂眾人言曰：「吾前為侯常侍用吏，陳君密持教還，而於外白署。

translations.literal: Lun said to the crowd: 'Formerly for Regular Attendant Hou I used an official; Lord Chen secretly held the instruction and returned it, and made the appointment himself from outside.
translations.idiomatic: Lun told the crowd how Shi had pocketed Hou's writ and reassigned the job outward—

sentences.zh: 比聞議者以此少之，此咎由故人畏憚強禦，陳君可謂善則稱君，過則稱己者也。

translations.literal: Recently I hear debaters fault him for this—the blame lies with me for fearing strong bullies; Lord Chen may be called one who attributes good to his lord and fault to himself.'
translations.idiomatic: fault belonged to Lun's cowardice, while Shi credited his superior and bore shame himself—

sentences.zh: 」寔固自引愆，聞者方歎息，由是天下服其德。

translations.literal: ' Shi firmly took the blame on himself; those who heard then sighed; thereby the realm admired his virtue.
translations.idiomatic: yet Shi publicly assumed guilt, moving listeners until the empire praised his decency.

sentences.zh: 司空黃瓊辟選理劇，補聞喜長，旬月，以開喪去官。

translations.literal: Minister of Works Huang Qiong recruited him for handling acute affairs; appointed magistrate of Wenxi; after a month, left office for mourning.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Qiong's Works ministry named him Wenxi magistrate for crisis duty; he quit within a month when mourning called.

sentences.zh: 復再遷除太丘長。

translations.literal: Again twice promoted, appointed magistrate of Taiqiu.
translations.idiomatic: Two further promotions made him magistrate of Taiqiu.

sentences.zh: 〈太丘，縣，屬沛國，故城在今亳州永城縣西北也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Taiqiu is a county belonging to the Pei kingdom; its old walled town is northwest of Yongcheng county in Bozhou today.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Taiqiu in Pei lay northwest of today's Yongcheng in Bozhou.〉

sentences.zh: 修德清靜，百姓以安。

translations.literal: He cultivated virtue with quiet purity; the hundred clans found peace.
translations.idiomatic: Quiet integrity brought the county peace.

sentences.zh: 鄰縣人戶歸附者，寔輒訓導譬解，發遣各令還本司官行部。

translations.literal: Households from neighboring counties who attached—Shi always instructed and explained, sent them off to each return to their original jurisdiction when the inspecting official toured.
translations.idiomatic: Refugees from other jurisdictions he coached and sent home when inspectors toured.

sentences.zh: 〈司官謂主司之官也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Inspecting official means the chief responsible official.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈"Inspecting official" denotes each county's proper chief.〉

sentences.zh: 吏慮有訟者，白欲禁之。

translations.literal: Clerks worried there would be litigants, reported wishing to forbid it.
translations.idiomatic: Staff feared lawsuits and urged a ban.

sentences.zh: 寔曰：「訟以求直，禁之理將何申？

translations.literal: Shi said: 'Litigation seeks straightness—if forbidden, how will principle be asserted?'
translations.idiomatic: Shi replied, "Suits seek justice—silencing them smothers truth."

sentences.zh: 其勿有所拘。

translations.literal: ' Do not constrain them in any way.
translations.idiomatic: Let no one obstruct petitioners.

sentences.zh: 」司官聞而歎息曰：「陳君所言若是，豈有怨於人乎？

translations.literal: ' The inspecting official heard and sighed: 'If Lord Chen's words are thus, how could there be resentment toward others?'
translations.idiomatic: The touring inspector sighed that Chen's fairness left no room for grudges—

sentences.zh: 」亦竟無訟者。

translations.literal: ' In the end there were no litigants either.
translations.idiomatic: and indeed no suits arose.

sentences.zh: 以沛相賦斂違法，及解印綬去，吏人追思之。

translations.literal: Because the chancellor of Pei exceeded lawful levies, he removed seal and ribbon and left; clerks and people long remembered him.
translations.idiomatic: When Pei's chancellor taxed illegally, Shi resigned his seal; clerks and folk never forgot him.

sentences.zh: 及後逮捕黨人，事亦連寔。

translations.literal: Later when the Partisan Purge arrested men, the affair also implicated Shi.
translations.idiomatic: When the faction lists swept the realm, Chen Shi's name landed on them.

sentences.zh: 餘人多逃避求免，寔曰：「吾不就獄，眾無所恃。

translations.literal: The others mostly fled and hid seeking exemption; Shi said: 'If I do not go to prison, the multitude has nothing to rely on.
translations.idiomatic: While others fled the arrest lists, Shi insisted that without his surrender the community would lose its moral anchor.

sentences.zh: 」乃請囚焉。

translations.literal: ' Thereupon he requested to be imprisoned.
translations.idiomatic: He volunteered for jail.

sentences.zh: 遇赦得出。

translations.literal: Meeting amnesty he was able to come out.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty freed him.

sentences.zh: 靈帝初，大將軍竇武辟以為掾屬。

translations.literal: Early in Emperor Ling's reign, Grand General Dou Wu recruited him as aide.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ling's early years brought recruitment from Grand General Dou Wu.

sentences.zh: 時中常侍張讓權傾天下。

translations.literal: At that time Regular Palace Attendant Zhang Rang's power tilted the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Eunuch Zhang Rang then overshadowed the empire.

sentences.zh: 讓父死，歸葬穎川，雖一郡畢至，而名士無往者，讓甚恥之，寔乃獨吊焉。

translations.literal: Rang's father died and was returned for burial in Yingchuan; although the whole commandery attended, no eminent gentlemen went—Rang was deeply shamed by this—Shi alone paid condolence.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Rang buried his father in Yingchuan, every official attended except the luminaries—until Chen Shi alone appeared, humbling Rang's clan.

sentences.zh: 乃後復誅黨人，讓感寔，故多所全宥。

translations.literal: Later when party men were again executed, Rang was grateful to Shi and therefore spared many.
translations.idiomatic: When the lists reopened, Rang remembered Chen Shi's grace and spared numerous scholars.

sentences.zh: 寔在鄉閭，平心率物。

translations.literal: Shi in village and neighborhood levelly led things.
translations.idiomatic: At home he treated neighbors with unvarying fairness.

sentences.zh: 其有爭訟，輒求判正，曉譬曲直，退無怨者。

translations.literal: If there were disputes, they always sought his judgment; he made clear right and wrong, and on withdrawal there were none who resented.
translations.idiomatic: Villagers brought every quarrel to him; he explained right from wrong so both sides left satisfied.

sentences.zh: 至乃歎曰：「寧為刑罰所加，不為陳君所短。

translations.literal: It reached the point that they sighed: 'Better suffer punishment inflicted [by the state] than be faulted by Lord Chen.
translations.idiomatic: People said they would rather face the magistrate's rod than disappoint Chen Shi.

sentences.zh: 」時歲荒民儉，有盜夜入其室，止於樑上。

translations.literal: ' At that time the year was famished and the people poor; a thief in the night entered his chamber and stopped on the beam.
translations.idiomatic: In a hunger year a burglar hid on his roof beam—

sentences.zh: 寔陰見，乃起自整拂，呼命子孫，正色訓之曰：「夫人不可不自勉。

translations.literal: Shi saw him in the dark, thereupon rose and straightened his clothes, called and ordered his sons and grandsons, and with grave mien instructed them, saying: 'A man must not fail to strive himself.
translations.idiomatic: Shi caught sight of him, rose, straightened his dress, gathered the family, and said every man must struggle toward virtue—

sentences.zh: 不善之人未必本惡，習以性成，遂至於此。

translations.literal: ' A man who is not good is not necessarily evil in root—habit forms nature, and he reaches this pass.
translations.idiomatic: bad character often comes from habit, not innate malice—

sentences.zh: 樑上君子者是矣！

translations.literal: The gentleman on the beam is a case in point!'
translations.idiomatic: as with the man on our beam."

sentences.zh: 」盜大驚，自投於地，稽顙歸罪。

translations.literal: ' The thief was greatly startled, threw himself to the ground, and knocked his forehead in confessing guilt.
translations.idiomatic: The thief slid down, kowtowing in shame.

sentences.zh: 寔徐譬之曰：「視君狀貌，不似惡人，宜深克己反善。

translations.literal: Shi slowly explained to him: 'Looking at your bearing and appearance, you do not resemble an evil man—you ought deeply to conquer yourself and return to goodness.
translations.idiomatic: Shi soothed him: his looks suggested reform remained possible—

sentences.zh: 然此當由貧困。

translations.literal: ' Yet this must come from poverty.
translations.idiomatic: poverty had driven him to theft.

sentences.zh: 」令遺絹二匹。

translations.literal: ' He ordered two bolts of silk be given him.
translations.idiomatic: Shi sent him off with two rolls of silk.

sentences.zh: 自是一縣無復盜竊。

translations.literal: From this the whole county had no more thievery.
translations.idiomatic: After that the county saw no more burglaries.

sentences.zh: 太尉楊賜、司徒陳耽，每拜公卿，羣僚畢賀，賜等常歎寔大位未登，愧於先之。

translations.literal: Grand Commandant Yang Ci and Minister of Education Chen Dan, whenever they were appointed duke or minister, the host of officials all congratulated them; Ci and the like always sighed that Shi's great post had not been reached, ashamed to precede him.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Ci and Chen Dan, on winning the highest posts, confessed shame that Chen Shi had not been promoted first.

sentences.zh: 及黨禁始解，大將軍何進、司徒袁隗遣人敦寔，

translations.literal: When the party prohibition first lifted, Grand General He Jin and Minister of Education Yuan Wei sent men earnestly to [persuade] Shi,
translations.idiomatic: When the ban ended, He Jin and Yuan Wei pressed envoys on Chen Shi—

sentences.zh: 〈敦，勸也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Dun means 'urge.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Dun means to press or exhort.〉

sentences.zh: 欲特表以不次之位。

translations.literal: wishing to recommend him in a special memorial to an out-of-sequence post.
translations.idiomatic: intending to recommend him for a rank that skipped the normal ladder.

sentences.zh: 寔乃謝使者曰：「寔久絕人事，飾巾待終而已。

translations.literal: Shi thereupon declined to the envoys, saying: 'I have long cut off human affairs; I only wait to wrap my headcloth for the end.'
translations.idiomatic: Shi told them he had left public life and only awaited death in the turban of retirement.

sentences.zh: 」時三公每缺，議者歸之，累見征命，遂不起，閉門懸車，棲□養老。

translations.literal: ' At that time whenever the Three Excellencies had a vacancy, debaters named him; he was repeatedly summoned by edict but in the end did not rise—closing his gate, suspending his carriage, dwelling in retirement to nourish old age.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever a ministership opened, opinion nominated Shi; edicts piled up but he bolted his gate, hung up his cart, and aged in seclusion.

sentences.zh: 中平四年，年八十四，卒於家。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Zhongping, at age eighty-four, he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home in Zhongping 4 at eighty-four.

sentences.zh: 何進遣使弔祭，海內赴者三萬餘人，制衰麻者以百數。

translations.literal: He Jin sent envoys to mourn and sacrifice; those who came from within the seas were more than thirty thousand; those in trimmed hemp mourning numbered in the hundreds.
translations.idiomatic: He Jin sponsored the rites; over thirty thousand mourners arrived, hundreds in full hemp.

sentences.zh: 共刊石立碑，謚為文范先生。

translations.literal: Together they cut stone and erected a stele, posthumously styling him Master Wenfan.
translations.idiomatic: They carved a stele and gave him the posthumous title Master of Patterned Exemplarity.

sentences.zh: 〈先賢行狀曰：「將軍何進遣官屬吊祠為謚。」〉

translations.literal: 〈The Xianxian xingzhuang says: 'General He Jin sent officials to mourn and sacrifice and assign a posthumous title.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Local records credit He Jin's office with the mourning service and posthumous style.〉

sentences.zh: 有六子，紀、諶最賢。

translations.literal: He had six sons; Ji and Chen were most worthy.
translations.idiomatic: Six sons survived; Chen Ji and Chen Chen topped them in talent.

sentences.zh: 紀字符方，亦以至德稱。

translations.literal: Ji, courtesy name Yuanfang, likewise was famed for consummate virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ji, styled Yuanfang, matched his father's moral fame.

sentences.zh: 兄弟孝養，閨門廱和，後進之士皆推慕其風。

translations.literal: The brothers filially supported [their elders]; the inner gate was harmonious; later-arriving scholars all pushed and admired their manner.
translations.idiomatic: The brothers' filial harmony drew younger scholars to emulate them.

sentences.zh: 及遭黨錮，發憤著書數萬言，號曰陳子。

translations.literal: When he encountered the Partisan Prohibition, he angrily authored several myriad characters of writing, titled Master Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Under the faction ban he vented in essays published as Master Chen.

sentences.zh: 黨禁解，四府並命，無所屈就。

translations.literal: When the party prohibition lifted, the four offices together ordered him; he bent to accept none.
translations.idiomatic: Four ministries summoned him when the ban lifted; he refused all.

sentences.zh: 遭父憂，每哀至，輒歐血絕氣，雖衰服已除，而積毀消瘠，殆將滅性。

translations.literal: Encountering mourning for his father, whenever grief arrived he always vomited blood and stopped breathing; although sackcloth mourning had been removed, accumulated wasting emaciation nearly extinguished his nature.
translations.idiomatic: Mourning his father he spat blood at each surge of grief; even after shed mourning he wasted nearly to death.

sentences.zh: 豫州刺史嘉其至行，表上尚書，圖像百城，以厲風俗。

translations.literal: The Inspector of Yuzhou praised his utmost conduct, memorialized to the Imperial Secretariat, and pictured him in the hundred cities to stimulate custom.
translations.idiomatic: Yuzhou's inspector memorialized his devotion and ordered his portrait hung in every county seat.

sentences.zh: 董卓入洛陽，乃使就家拜五官中郎將，不得已，到京師，遷侍中。

translations.literal: When Dong Zhuo entered Luoyang, he had him at home appointed General of the Household for All Purposes; unable to refuse, he reached the capital and was promoted palace attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhuo's entry forced him to accept General of the Household, then palace attendant in the capital.

sentences.zh: 出為平原相，往謁卓，時欲徙都長安。

translations.literal: Going out as chancellor of Pingyuan, he went to pay respects to Zhuo; at that time [Zhuo] wished to move the capital to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: As Pingyuan chancellor he called on Zhuo, who meant to move the court west.

sentences.zh: 乃謂紀曰：「三輔平敞，四面險固，土地肥美，號為陸海。

translations.literal: Thereupon he said to Ji: 'The three adjuncts are flat and open, defended on four sides; the soil is fat and beautiful, called dry land and sea.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuo argued the Guanzhong heartland was fertile, defensible, the proverbial land-and-sea granary—

sentences.zh: 〈《前書》曰，東方朔曰：「三輔之地，南有江、淮，北有河、渭，汧、隴以東，商、洛以西，厥壤肥饒，此所謂天府陸海之地。」〉

translations.literal: 〈The earlier Han book says, Dongfang Shuo said: 'The land of the three adjuncts has south of it the Yangzi and Huai, north of it the Yellow and Wei rivers; east of Qian and Long, west of Shang and Luo—its soil is rich and fertile—this is what is called the Storehouse of Heaven and dry land and sea.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Han sources quote Dongfang Shuo's praise of Guanzhong's natural fortress and fertility.〉

sentences.zh: 今關東兵起，恐洛陽不可久居。

translations.literal: Now east-of-pass armies rise; I fear Luoyang cannot long be dwelt in.
translations.idiomatic: With coalition armies east of the pass, Luoyang could not hold—

sentences.zh: 長安猶有宮室，今欲西遷何如？

translations.literal: Chang'an still has palace buildings; now if we wish to remove west, how about it?
translations.idiomatic: while Chang'an still offered palaces for a western move.

sentences.zh: 」紀曰：「天下有道，守在四夷。

translations.literal: ' Ji said: 'When the realm has the Way, defense lies in the four Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ji answered that virtuous rule secures the realm through frontier allies—

sentences.zh: 〈《左傳》曰，楚沈尹戍曰「古者天子守在四夷。

translations.literal: 〈The Zuoshi zhuan says, Shen Yin Xu of Chu said: 'In antiquity the Son of Heaven's defense lay in the four Yi.
translations.idiomatic: 〈The Zuo Tradition glosses "defense in the four Yi"—

sentences.zh: 天子卑，守在諸侯」也。〉

translations.literal: When the Son of Heaven is base, defense lies in the feudal lords.'〉
translations.idiomatic: or in vassal armies when the throne weakens.'〉

sentences.zh: 宜修德政，以懷不附。

translations.literal: It is fitting to cultivate virtuous government to embrace those not attached.
translations.idiomatic: Better win dissidents through humane rule—

sentences.zh: 遷移至尊，誠計之末者。

translations.literal: To shift the supreme dignity is truly the least of plans.
translations.idiomatic: while relocating the emperor ranks last among strategies.

sentences.zh: 愚以公宜事委公卿，專精外任。

translations.literal: I foolishly think you ought to entrust affairs to the dukes and ministers and concentrate on frontier command.
translations.idiomatic: Shi urged Zhuo to delegate civil rule and focus on military command—

sentences.zh: 其有違命，則威之以武。

translations.literal: If there are those who disobey orders, then awe them with arms.
translations.idiomatic: using force only against open rebels.

sentences.zh: 今關東兵起，民不堪命。

translations.literal: Now east-of-pass armies rise; the people cannot bear the commands.
translations.idiomatic: Eastern levies already crushed the people—

sentences.zh: 若謙遠朝政，率師討伐，則塗炭之民，庶幾可全。

translations.literal: If you modestly distance yourself from court governance and lead troops to punish and attack, then the people [trampled to] mud and charcoal may by a hair's breadth be preserved.
translations.idiomatic: personal command against the coalition might yet spare the commoners—

sentences.zh: 若欲徙萬乘以自安，將有累卵之危，崢嶸之險也。」

translations.literal: If you wish to move the myriad carriages to secure yourself, there will be the peril of stacked eggs, the danger of precarious heights.'
translations.idiomatic: whereas hauling the court west stacked disaster like eggs on a cliff."

sentences.zh: 〈累卵，解見皇后紀。

translations.literal: 〈Stacked eggs—explanation seen in the annals of the empresses.
translations.idiomatic: 〈"Stacked eggs" recalls the empresses' annals—

sentences.zh: 崢音士耕反。〉

translations.literal: Zheng is read shi-geng fan.'〉
translations.idiomatic: zheng is read with the fanqie shi–geng.〉

sentences.zh: 卓意甚忤，而敬紀名行，無所復言。

translations.literal: Zhuo's intent was very offended, yet he respected Ji's name and conduct, and had nothing further to say.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuo bridled but respected Chen Ji too much to argue.

sentences.zh: 時議欲以為司徒，紀見禍亂方作，不復辨嚴，

translations.literal: At that time deliberation wished to make him Minister of Education; Ji saw disaster and chaos about to arise and no longer organized luggage,
translations.idiomatic: When ministers wished to install Chen Ji as Minister of Education, he saw turmoil coming and never bothered packing—

sentences.zh: 〈嚴讀曰裝也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Yan is read as zhuang.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Yan here reads as zhuang, baggage.〉

sentences.zh: 實時之郡。

translations.literal: and in reality went at once to the commandery.
translations.idiomatic: fleeing straight to his commandery post.

sentences.zh: 璽書追拜太僕，又征為尚書令。

translations.literal: Sealed edict pursued appointment as Grand Coachman, again summoning him as Imperial Secretariat Director.
translations.idiomatic: Imperial messengers chased him with Grand Coachman and Secretariat Director appointments.

sentences.zh: 建安初，袁紹為太尉，讓於紀；

translations.literal: Early in Jian'an, Yuan Shao was Grand Commandant and yielded to Ji;
translations.idiomatic: In early Jian'an Yuan Shao held Grand Commandant and offered the title to Chen Ji—

sentences.zh: 紀不受，拜大鴻臚。

translations.literal: Ji did not accept; he was appointed Grand Herald.
translations.idiomatic: Ji refused, accepting only Grand Herald.

sentences.zh: 年七十一，卒於官。

translations.literal: At seventy-one he died in office.
translations.idiomatic: He died in office at seventy-one.

sentences.zh: 子羣，為魏司空。

translations.literal: His son was Qun, who became Minister of Works for Wei.
translations.idiomatic: His son Chen Qun became Wei Minister of Works.

sentences.zh: 〈羣字長文。

translations.literal: 〈Qun's courtesy name is Changwen.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Qun bore the style Changwen—

sentences.zh: 魏志曰「魯國孔融才高倨傲，年在羣、紀之閒，先與[紀友，後與]羣交，更為紀拜，由是顯名」也。〉

translations.literal: The Wei zhi says: 'Kong Rong of Lu was talented and arrogant; his age fell between Qun and Ji; first he befriended Ji, later associated with Qun, and changed to bow to Ji—thereby his name became manifest.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Wei histories note Kong Rong respecting both Chen brothers, shifting friendship from elder to younger.〉

sentences.zh: 天下以為公慙卿，卿慙長。

translations.literal: The realm thought the duke ashamed before the minister, the minister ashamed before the elder.
translations.idiomatic: Wits quipped that the Excellency blushed before the minister and the minister before the patriarch.

sentences.zh: 弟諶，字季方。

translations.literal: The younger brother was Chen, courtesy name Jifang.
translations.idiomatic: Younger brother Chen Chen styled Jifang.

sentences.zh: 與紀齊德同行，父子並著高名，時號三君。

translations.literal: With Ji they matched virtue and conduct; father and sons together bore lofty names; the age called them the Three Lords.
translations.idiomatic: He rivaled Chen Ji in virtue; father and sons were hailed as the Three Paragons.

sentences.zh: 每宰府辟召，常同時旌命，羔鴈成羣，

translations.literal: Whenever ministerial offices summoned them, they were always enfeoffed together; lambs and wild geese came in flocks,
translations.idiomatic: Bureaus courted them in tandem—gift lambs and geese arrived in crowds—

sentences.zh: 〈古者諸侯朝天子，卿執羔，大夫執鴈，士執雉。

translations.literal: 〈In antiquity when feudal lords courted the Son of Heaven, ministers presented lambs, grandees wild geese, gentlemen pheasants.
translations.idiomatic: 〈Classical audiences required lambs from ministers, geese from grandees—

sentences.zh: 成羣言眾多也。〉

translations.literal: Flocks means numerous.'〉
translations.idiomatic: hence the metaphor of flocking gifts.〉

sentences.zh: 當世者靡不榮之。

translations.literal: Those eminent in the age none failed to honor them.
translations.idiomatic: Contemporaries venerated them without exception.

sentences.zh: 諶早終。

translations.literal: Chen died early.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Chen died young.

sentences.zh: 〈先賢行狀曰：「豫州百城，皆圖書寔、紀、諶形像焉。」〉

translations.literal: 〈The Xianxian xingzhuang says: 'The hundred cities of Yuzhou all painted portraits of Shi, Ji, and Chen.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Local records say Yuzhou hung portraits of all three Chens.〉

sentences.zh: 論曰：漢自中世以下，閹豎□恣，故俗遂以遁身矯絜放言為高。

translations.literal: The judgment says: From mid-Han downward, the eunuchs [missing graph] indulged themselves; therefore custom came to treat withdrawal, feigned purity, and reckless speech as lofty.
translations.idiomatic: Mid-Han eunuchs ran wild; fashion praised recluses who flaunted purity and brazen talk.

sentences.zh: 〈放肆其言，不拘節制也。

translations.literal: 〈Reckless speech lets words run free without restraint.
translations.idiomatic: 〈"Reckless speech" means unbridled words—

sentences.zh: 論語曰：「隱居放言。」〉

translations.literal: The Lunyu says: 'Living in reclusion, letting speech run.'〉
translations.idiomatic: the Analects pairing it with eremitic life.〉

sentences.zh: 士有不談此者，則芸夫牧豎已叫呼之矣。

translations.literal: If a gentleman did not discourse on this, then weeders and swineherds already shouted at him.
translations.idiomatic: Any scholar who spurned that pose faced mockery from the roughest laborers.

sentences.zh: 〈叫呼，譏笑之也。

translations.literal: 〈Shouting means ridiculing.
translations.idiomatic: 〈To shout at someone here means to jeer—

sentences.zh: 芸，除草也。〉

translations.literal: Yun means weeding.'〉
translations.idiomatic: yun means hoeing weeds.〉

sentences.zh: 故時政彌惛，而其風愈往。

translations.literal: Therefore governance grew darker and that fashion ran stronger.
translations.idiomatic: So policy rotted while pose-striking spread.

sentences.zh: 唯陳先生進退之節，必可度也。

translations.literal: Only Master Chen's norms of advancement and withdrawal could surely be measured.
translations.idiomatic: Only Chen Shi navigated office and retirement with measurable principle.

sentences.zh: 據於德故物不犯，安於仁故不離羣，行成乎身而道訓天下，故凶邪不能以權奪，王公不能以貴驕，所以聲教廢於上，而風俗清乎下也。

translations.literal: Relying on virtue therefore things did not violate him; resting in benevolence therefore he did not leave the flock; conduct completing in his person yet the Way instructing the realm—therefore the vicious could not wrest him by craft, kings and dukes could not overbear him with nobility—therefore when acoustic teaching failed above, custom remained pure below.
translations.idiomatic: Grounded in virtue, nothing could stain him; anchored in humanity, he never abandoned community; personal integrity broadcast the Way so neither intrigue nor rank could sway him—keeping local morals clean even when court rhetoric collapsed.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：二李師淑，陳君友皓。

translations.literal: The encomium says: The two Lis took Shu as teacher; Lord Chen befriended Hao.
translations.idiomatic: The stanza pairs Li Gu and Li Ying with Xun Shu, Han Shao's virtue, Zhong Hao's friendship—

sentences.zh: 韓韶就吏，贏寇懷道。

translations.literal: Han Shao took office; Ying bandits embraced his Way.
translations.idiomatic: Han Shao fed refugees while brigands respected his name.

sentences.zh: 太丘奧廣，模我彝倫。

translations.literal: Taiqiu vast and deep—pattern for our constant norms.
translations.idiomatic: Taiqiu's magnanimity modeled enduring ethics.

sentences.zh: 曾是淵軌，薄夫以淳。

translations.literal: Such were the deep tracks—petty men grew honest.
translations.idiomatic: Those deep ruts turned cynics sincere.

sentences.zh: 〈曾之言則也。〉

translations.literal: 〈Zeng means 'then.'〉
translations.idiomatic: 〈Zeng functions as "thus" or "then."〉

sentences.zh: 慶基既啟，有蔚穎濱，二方承則，八慈繼塵。

translations.literal: Felicitous foundations opened; brilliance clustered by the Ying shore—the two Fang heirs carried the norm, the Eight C Kindnesses followed in the dust.
translations.idiomatic: Their lineage flourished along the Ying River: Chen Yuanfang and Chen Jifang upheld their father's example while Xun Shu's eight sons—all bearing the courtesy element Ci—trooped after them as admirers.

sentences.zh: 〈二方，元方﹑季方也。

translations.literal: 〈The two Fang are Yuanfang and Jifang.
translations.idiomatic: 〈"Two Fang" names the Chen brothers—

sentences.zh: 荀淑八子，皆以慈為字，見荀氏家傳也。

translations.literal: Xun Shu's eight sons all took ci 'kindness' as an element in their courtesy names—see the Xun clan household biography.'〉
translations.idiomatic: The family chronicle records that each of Xun Shu's eight sons embedded the character for kindness in his courtesy name.'〉
